title: Claude Certified Architect – Foundations: Practice Exam

questions.task_desc: Multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff
questions.stem: Production data shows that in 12% of cases, your agent skips `get_customer` entirely and calls `lookup_order` using only the customer's stated name, occasionally leading to misidentified accounts and incorrect refunds. What change would most effectively address this reliability issue?
options: Implement a routing classifier that analyzes each request and enables only the subset of tools appropriate for that request type.
Add few-shot examples showing the agent always calling `get_customer` first, even when customers volunteer order details.
Enhance the system prompt to state that customer verification via `get_customer` is mandatory before any order operations.
Add a programmatic prerequisite that blocks `lookup_order` and `process_refund` calls until `get_customer` has returned a verified customer ID.
questions.explanation: When a specific tool sequence is required for critical business logic, enforcing the prerequisite programmatically—blocking the order-lookup and refund tools until a verified customer ID has been returned—provides deterministic guarantees and reliably closes the 12% failure gap. Strengthening the system prompt to mandate verification and adding few-shot examples of always calling the verification tool first both depend on probabilistic LLM compliance, so they reduce but cannot eliminate the skips. A routing classifier that enables only a subset of tools addresses which tools are available for a request type, not the required ordering between tools, so it would not stop the agent from looking up an order using only a stated name.

questions.task_desc: Tool interface design with clear descriptions
questions.stem: Production logs show the agent frequently calls `get_customer` when users ask about orders, instead of calling `lookup_order`. Both tools have minimal descriptions and accept similar identifier formats. What's the most effective first step to improve tool selection reliability?
options: Consolidate both tools into a single `lookup_entity` tool that accepts any identifier and internally determines which backend to query.
Add few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection patterns, with 5-8 examples.
Implement a routing layer that parses user input before each turn and pre-selects the appropriate tool based on detected keywords.
Expand each tool's description to include input formats it handles, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries explaining when to use it versus similar tools.
questions.explanation: Tool descriptions are the primary signal the model uses to choose a tool, so the root cause here is the two tools' minimal, overlapping descriptions; enriching each description with input formats, example queries, edge cases, and explicit "use this versus that" boundaries directly disambiguates them and is a low-effort first step. Adding few-shot selection examples to the system prompt can help but is secondary, adds token overhead, and leaves the ambiguous descriptions unfixed. A keyword-based routing layer is brittle and over-engineered, discarding the model's own tool-selection ability, while merging the two tools into a single entity-lookup tool is a larger architectural change than a "first step" warrants and sacrifices the semantic clarity that helps selection.

questions.stem: Your agent achieves 55% first-contact resolution, well below the 80% target. Logs show it escalates straightforward cases while attempting to autonomously handle complex situations requiring policy exceptions. What's the most effective way to improve escalation calibration?
options: Add explicit escalation criteria to your system prompt with few-shot examples demonstrating when to escalate versus resolve autonomously.
Implement sentiment analysis to detect customer frustration levels and automatically escalate when negative sentiment exceeds a threshold.
Deploy a separate classifier model trained on historical tickets to predict which requests need escalation before the main agent begins processing.
Have the agent self-report a confidence score (1-10) before each response and automatically route requests to humans when confidence falls below a threshold.
questions.explanation: The logs reveal the root cause is an unclear decision boundary: the agent escalates easy cases and tackles hard ones it shouldn't, so the direct fix is to give it explicit, criteria-based guidance plus few-shot examples that model exactly when to escalate versus resolve. Relying on the agent's self-reported confidence score is unreliable because LLM confidence is poorly calibrated and doesn't track true case difficulty. Training and deploying a separate escalation-prediction classifier is heavy over-engineering when the problem is simply undefined boundaries, and detecting customer frustration via sentiment targets emotional tone rather than the case-complexity misjudgment shown in the logs.

questions.stem: You want to create a custom `/review` slash command that runs your team's standard code review checklist and should be available to every developer when they clone or pull the repository. Where should you create this command file?
options: In `~/.claude/commands/` in each developer's home directory
In a `.claude/config.json` file with a `commands` array
In the `.claude/commands/` directory in the project repository
In the `CLAUDE.md` file at the project root
questions.explanation: Project-scoped custom slash commands live in the `.claude/commands/` directory inside the repository, where each command is a Markdown file that gets version-controlled and is automatically available to everyone who clones or pulls the repo — making the project-directory choice correct. Placing the file in the developer's home directory (`~/.claude/commands/`) only creates a personal, machine-local command that is not shared with the team. The project-root instructions file (`CLAUDE.md`) provides guidance and context, not executable slash-command definitions, and a `config.json` with a `commands` array is not a real mechanism for defining slash commands.

questions.stem: You've been assigned to restructure a monolithic application into microservices. This will involve changes across dozens of files and requires decisions about service boundaries and module dependencies. Which approach should you take?
options: Enter plan mode to explore the codebase, understand dependencies, and design an implementation approach before making changes.
Use direct execution with comprehensive upfront instructions detailing exactly how each service should be structured.
Begin in direct execution mode and only switch to plan mode if you encounter unexpected complexity during implementation.
Start with direct execution and make changes incrementally, letting the implementation reveal the natural service boundaries.
questions.explanation: Plan mode is intended precisely for complex, large-scale work that spans many files and hinges on architectural decisions with multiple valid approaches, so exploring the codebase and designing an approach before editing is the right move here. Diving straight into incremental changes and hoping the service boundaries reveal themselves invites costly rework when dependencies surface late. Supplying detailed upfront instructions presumes you already know the correct structure without having explored the code, and deferring planning until unexpected complexity appears ignores that the complexity is already known from the outset.

questions.task_desc: Path-specific rules for conditional convention loading
questions.stem: Your codebase has distinct areas with different conventions. Test files are spread throughout the codebase alongside the code they test. You want all tests to follow the same conventions regardless of location. What's the most maintainable way to ensure Claude automatically applies the correct conventions when generating code?
options: Create rule files in `.claude/rules/` with YAML frontmatter specifying glob patterns to conditionally apply conventions based on file paths.
Create skills in `.claude/skills/` for each code type that include the relevant conventions in their `SKILL.md` files.
Consolidate all conventions in the root `CLAUDE.md` file under headers for each area, relying on Claude to infer which section applies.
Place a separate `CLAUDE.md` file in each subdirectory containing that area's specific conventions.
questions.explanation: The purpose-built mechanism for this is rule files in `.claude/rules/` whose frontmatter declares glob patterns (e.g. `**/*.test.tsx`); conventions then attach automatically to any matching file no matter where it lives, which is exactly what "tests spread throughout the codebase" needs. Putting everything under headers in the root CLAUDE.md forces Claude to infer which section applies rather than matching explicitly, and skills must be invoked/triggered rather than applied automatically by path. A separate CLAUDE.md in each subdirectory is unmaintainable when the target files are scattered across many directories, since it requires duplicating the same conventions everywhere.

questions.stem: After running on "impact of AI on creative industries," each subagent completes successfully but the final reports cover only visual arts, missing music, writing, and film production. The coordinator decomposed the topic into: "AI in digital art creation," "AI in graphic design," and "AI in photography." What is the most likely root cause?
options: The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps in the findings it receives from other agents.
The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough and need to be expanded to cover more creative industry sectors.
The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in subagent assignments that don't cover all relevant domains.
The document analysis agent is filtering out sources related to non-visual creative industries due to overly restrictive relevance criteria.
questions.explanation: The stem shows the coordinator split "creative industries" into three narrow visual-arts subtopics (digital art, graphic design, photography), so music, writing, and film were never assigned to any subagent — this decomposition failure is the root cause, and each subagent still "completed successfully" because they correctly executed the limited tasks they were given. The explanation blaming the synthesis agent for missing gap-detection is wrong because no amount of gap-checking recovers domains that were never researched. The theories blaming the search agent's query breadth or the document analyzer's relevance filtering are also wrong, since the missing coverage traces directly to the assignments themselves, not to how any subagent searched or filtered sources.

questions.task_desc: Structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.stem: The web search subagent times out while researching a complex topic. You need to design how this failure information flows back to the coordinator agent. Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent recovery?
options: Catch the timeout within the subagent and return an empty result set marked as successful.
Propagate the timeout exception directly to a top-level handler that terminates the entire research workflow.
Return structured error context to the coordinator including the failure type, the attempted query, any partial results, and potential alternative approaches.
Implement automatic retry logic with exponential backoff within the subagent, returning a generic "search unavailable" status only after all retries are exhausted.
questions.explanation: Returning structured error context — the failure type, the attempted query, any partial results, and possible alternative approaches — gives the coordinator the rich information it needs to decide how to recover intelligently. Hiding everything behind a generic "search unavailable" status (even after in-subagent retries) discards that context, and silently returning an empty result marked as successful suppresses the failure entirely so no recovery is possible. Propagating the raw timeout up to a handler that terminates the whole workflow throws away recoverable work and over-reacts to a single subagent failure.

questions.task_desc: Tool distribution across agents and tool_choice
questions.stem: The synthesis agent frequently needs to verify specific claims while combining findings. Currently, this creates 2-3 round trips per task, increasing latency by 40%. 85% of verifications are simple fact-checks; 15% require deeper investigation. What's the most effective approach to reduce overhead while maintaining reliability?
options: Have the web search agent proactively cache extra context around each source during initial research, anticipating what the synthesis agent might need to verify.
Have the synthesis agent accumulate all verification needs and return them as a batch to the coordinator at the end of its pass.
Give the synthesis agent a scoped `verify_fact` tool for simple lookups, while complex verifications continue delegating to the web search agent through the coordinator.
Give the synthesis agent access to all web search tools so it can handle any verification need directly without round-trips.
questions.explanation: Giving the synthesis agent a narrowly scoped fact-check tool for the 85% common case while still routing the 15% of complex verifications through the coordinator applies least-privilege tool design: it eliminates most round-trips inline without bloating the agent or its context. Accumulating all verifications into an end-of-pass batch introduces a blocking dependency and cannot support verifying claims as they are combined. Handing the synthesis agent every web-search tool over-provisions it and enlarges its context and failure surface, while proactively caching extra source context relies on speculation about what will be needed and adds waste when guesses miss.

questions.stem: Your pipeline script runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Logs indicate Claude Code is waiting for interactive input. What's the correct approach?
options: Add the `-p` flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
Redirect stdin from `/dev/null`: `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues" < /dev/null`
Add the `--batch` flag: `claude --batch "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
Set the environment variable `CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true` before running the command
questions.explanation: The `-p` (or `--print`) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code non-interactively, printing the response and exiting instead of launching an interactive session that waits for input — exactly what a CI/CD job needs. The choices invoking a `CLAUDE_HEADLESS` environment variable and a `--batch` flag both reference features that do not exist in Claude Code. Redirecting stdin from `/dev/null` is an unreliable Unix workaround that does not properly invoke Claude Code's non-interactive print mode.

questions.stem: Your team wants to reduce API costs. Two workflows: (1) a blocking pre-merge check that must complete before developers can merge, and (2) a technical debt report generated overnight for review the next morning. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for 50% cost savings. How should you evaluate this proposal?
options: Switch both workflows to batch processing with status polling to check for completion.
Keep real-time calls for both workflows to avoid batch result ordering issues.
Switch both to batch processing with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take too long.
Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep real-time calls for pre-merge checks.
questions.explanation: The Message Batches API delivers a 50% cost reduction but processes asynchronously with no low-latency guarantee: most batches finish within an hour, yet requests can take up to the 24-hour expiration window. That makes it fine for the overnight technical-debt report (which is only read the next morning) but unacceptable for a blocking pre-merge gate that developers wait on, so the right move is to batch only the report and keep real-time calls for the merge check. Switching both to batch (even with polling) stalls merges, and keeping both real-time needlessly forgoes savings on the report. The idea that batching must be avoided because of result ordering is a misconception, since results are correlated back to requests via the custom_id field; and a "timeout fallback to real-time" is impractical because batches have no per-request SLA to time out against and you'd end up paying for both paths.

questions.stem: A pull request modifies 14 files across the stock tracking module. Your single-pass review produces inconsistent results: detailed feedback for some files, superficial comments for others, obvious bugs missed, and contradictory feedback. How should you restructure the review?
options: Run three independent review passes on the full PR and only flag issues that appear in at least two of the three runs.
Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window to give all 14 files adequate attention in one pass.
Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration-focused pass examining cross-file data flow.
Require developers to split large PRs into smaller submissions of 3-4 files before the automated review runs.
questions.explanation: Decomposing the review into focused passes—analyzing each file individually for local defects, then a dedicated integration pass over cross-file data flow—directly counters the attention dilution that causes uneven, contradictory single-pass output, giving every file consistent scrutiny. Making developers pre-split large PRs only offloads the problem onto humans without improving the review system itself, and moving to a larger-context model addresses capacity rather than the real issue of attention quality being spread thin. Requiring an issue to appear in at least two of three independent runs hurts recall, since genuine bugs that are only caught intermittently would be filtered out by the consensus requirement.

questions.task_desc: Agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.stem:
You are building a customer support agent using the Claude Agent SDK. The agent processes billing disputes by calling tools like `lookup_order`, `get_invoice`, and `process_refund`. A code review notes that after each tool call, your loop checks whether Claude's response text contains the phrase "I have completed" to decide whether to stop.

What is the primary problem with this loop termination approach?
options: The agent will never terminate because `process_refund` always returns a success message that prevents "I have completed" from appearing.
Relying on natural language signals in the assistant's text is unreliable; the correct approach is to inspect `stop_reason` and only terminate when it equals `"end_turn"`.
The loop should terminate as soon as any tool call fails, since continuing after a failure will corrupt the conversation history.
Checking response text is only valid in synchronous mode; you must use a callback handler for proper loop control in async contexts.
questions.explanation: The canonical way to control an agentic tool-use loop is to inspect the API response's `stop_reason` field: continue iterating while it is `"tool_use"` and terminate when it is `"end_turn"` (or another terminal state). Matching on a natural-language phrase like "I have completed" is brittle because Claude's wording varies across responses, models, and prompt revisions, so it can never be a dependable stop condition. The claim that a refund success message would loop forever and the claim that any tool failure must halt the loop both rest on false assumptions about how tool results relate to response text, and the sync-vs-async callback distinction is unrelated to how termination is actually decided.

questions.task_desc: Agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.stem:
A developer productivity agent investigates bug reports by calling `search_codebase`, `read_file`, and `run_tests`. During a code review, a colleague proposes adding an iteration cap: if the loop has not finished after 15 tool calls, terminate with a generic "investigation incomplete" response.

What is the correct characterization of this design?
options: The iteration cap should be replaced with a time-based timeout, since token counts are a more reliable measure of completion than iteration count.
Iteration caps are a reasonable safety boundary for long-running tasks but should not be the primary termination mechanism; `stop_reason: "end_turn"` remains the authoritative signal.
Iteration caps are the recommended primary stopping mechanism because they prevent runaway costs in production.
Iteration caps are unnecessary if the system prompt instructs the agent to always request only the minimum tools needed.
questions.explanation: In an agentic loop the model keeps returning `stop_reason: "tool_use"` while it needs tools and signals completion with `stop_reason: "end_turn"`, so that stop_reason is the authoritative termination signal; an iteration cap is a sensible backstop against runaway loops but making it the primary stopping mechanism can truncate legitimate multi-step investigations, which is exactly the balanced view the correct choice states. Treating a cap as the recommended primary mechanism is wrong for that reason. Swapping in a time-based timeout is also wrong, and its justification is incoherent since token counts have nothing to do with a wall-clock timeout. Claiming caps are unnecessary given a minimal-tools system prompt conflates prompt guidance with loop control, since even a well-prompted agent may need many tool calls for a complex bug investigation.

questions.task_desc: Agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.stem:
Your multi-agent research system's agentic loop appends each tool result to the conversation history before sending the next request. A teammate suggests instead storing all tool results in a separate database and providing only a summary to the model at each iteration, rather than the full result.

Under what condition would this change most likely degrade agent performance?
options: When the number of tool calls per session exceeds 10, since larger histories slow down the API.
When the tool results contain binary data such as images or file attachments.
When tools return results faster than 200ms, making history appending redundant.
When the model needs to reason across multiple tool results simultaneously to determine its next action, since summaries may omit details required for that reasoning.
questions.explanation: An agentic loop works because full tool results stay in the conversation history, letting the model reason over everything it has already gathered before choosing its next action; replacing those results with summaries risks dropping field values, error codes, or conditional details, which most hurts when the model must synthesize across several results at once. That is why the choice about needing to reason over multiple results simultaneously is correct. The binary-data option is a real handling concern but not the primary performance risk for text-based research, and the claims about a 10-call threshold slowing the API or sub-200ms results making history redundant are not genuine failure modes of this architecture.

questions.stem:
Your multi-agent research system uses a coordinator that always routes every query through all four subagents (web search, document analysis, synthesis, and report generation) regardless of query complexity. For a simple factual question like "What year was the Anthropic API released?", the system takes 45 seconds and incurs unnecessary cost.

What design change most directly addresses this?
options: Replace the coordinator with a static routing table that maps query keywords to specific subagent pipelines.
Have each subagent evaluate its own relevance to the current query and self-select into or out of the pipeline.
Design the coordinator to dynamically select which subagents to invoke based on query complexity and type, rather than always routing through the full pipeline.
Reduce the number of subagents from four to two by merging web search and document analysis into a single "retrieval" agent.
questions.explanation: The root problem is that the coordinator runs the full four-agent pipeline for every query regardless of complexity, so the most direct fix is to give the coordinator adaptive routing logic that evaluates each query's type and complexity and invokes only the subagents actually needed. The static keyword-to-pipeline mapping trades adaptive judgment for a brittle lookup table that misclassifies novel or ambiguous queries. Pushing the decision into each subagent so they self-select breaks the hub-and-spoke coordinator pattern, scatters control logic, and hurts observability. Merging four agents into two simply removes capability without addressing the real issue, which is that routing is unconditional rather than query-aware.

questions.stem:
A coordinator agent in your research system has delegated document analysis to a subagent. After the subagent finishes, the coordinator notices the findings are incomplete: the subagent covered only three of the five specified sources. The coordinator needs to get the remaining two sources analyzed.

What is the correct approach for re-delegating this work?
options: Let the coordinator generate its own analysis of the remaining two sources directly, rather than spawning another subagent delegation.
Invoke the synthesis agent with the partial findings and instruct it to infer what the missing sources likely contain based on patterns from the three completed analyses.
Send all five sources again to a new document analysis subagent instance, which will re-analyze the three already-completed sources along with the two missing ones.
The coordinator should re-invoke the document analysis subagent with an explicit prompt specifying only the two missing sources, including the previously completed findings as context.
questions.explanation: A coordinator's job is to evaluate subagent output for gaps and re-delegate targeted work, so the best move is to re-invoke the document-analysis subagent with a scoped prompt covering only the two unanalyzed sources while passing the three completed analyses along as context. Handing the gap to a synthesis step and asking it to guess the missing sources' contents would fabricate findings and destroy accuracy. Resending all five sources for a full re-analysis needlessly repeats work already done, and having the coordinator analyze the sources itself abandons the specialized subagent and defeats the coordinator-subagent pattern.

questions.stem:
You are building a coordinator that delegates research tasks to subagents using the `Task` tool. When you test the system, the coordinator cannot invoke any subagents. Reviewing the coordinator's `AgentDefinition`, you notice the `allowedTools` field is set to `["web_search", "read_document"]`.

What is the most likely cause of the failure?
options: The `Task` tool requires an explicit `subagent_endpoint` configuration before it can be invoked.
Subagent invocation requires the coordinator to be running in plan mode rather than direct execution mode.
`"Task"` is not included in the coordinator's `allowedTools`, so it cannot spawn subagents.
The coordinator's system prompt does not include instructions to use the `Task` tool, so the model never attempts to call it.
questions.explanation: The `Task` tool is the mechanism for spawning subagents in the Claude Agent SDK, so a coordinator can only invoke subagents when `"Task"` is explicitly included in its `allowedTools`; here the field lists only `web_search` and `read_document`, which is exactly why spawning fails. The choice invoking a `subagent_endpoint` configuration is wrong because no such parameter exists. Blaming the system prompt for lacking usage instructions confuses prompting with capability gating — even a well-prompted coordinator cannot call a tool absent from its allowed set. And requiring plan mode is incorrect, since subagent spawning is independent of plan versus direct execution mode.

questions.stem:
Your multi-agent research system has a web search subagent that consistently returns findings without identifying which sources correspond to which claims. When the synthesis agent receives these findings, it cannot produce properly cited reports.

What is the correct fix during context passing from the web search subagent to the synthesis agent?
options: Have the coordinator concatenate all subagent outputs into a single text block before forwarding to synthesis, since synthesis will extract citations naturally.

Use structured data formats that separate claim content from metadata (source URLs, publication dates, page numbers) in the subagent's output, and include this structure when passing context to the synthesis agent.
Instruct the synthesis agent to run its own web searches to re-locate the original sources.
Configure the web search subagent to return only source URLs, and have the synthesis agent re-read each source to reconstruct the findings.
questions.explanation: The correct fix is to have the search subagent emit structured output that explicitly binds each claim to its metadata (source URL, date, page), so the claim-to-source mapping survives the handoff to the synthesis agent; unstructured text is where citation associations get lost. Having the synthesis agent re-run its own searches introduces redundant work and may surface different results, while concatenating all subagent output into one text block does nothing to preserve the mappings and assumes synthesis can recover citations that were never encoded. Returning only URLs and forcing the synthesis agent to re-read every source discards the findings the subagent already produced and duplicates retrieval work.

questions.stem: A coordinator needs to research three independent subtopics in parallel: market trends, competitor analysis, and regulatory environment. Each requires a separate web search subagent. How should the coordinator spawn these subagents to maximize throughput?
options: Route all three subtopics through a single subagent sequentially, sharing context between them to reduce total memory usage.
Emit all three `Task` tool calls in a single coordinator response, which allows the subagents to run in parallel.
Spawn the subtopics sequentially: start the first subagent, wait for its result, then start the second, and so on, to avoid context conflicts.
Use a single subagent with three separate prompts in sequence, passing prior results as context for each subsequent prompt.
questions.explanation: To maximize throughput on independent subtopics, the coordinator should issue all three `Task` tool calls within a single response; the Agent SDK dispatches such calls concurrently, so the three web-search subagents run in parallel. Spawning them one at a time and waiting for each result adds needless serial latency, and funneling everything through a single subagent (whether framed as reducing memory use or as chained prompts passing prior results forward) collapses three independent jobs into one sequential process and eliminates parallelism. Chaining also wrongly assumes a dependency between subtopics that the scenario never states.

questions.task_desc: Multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff
questions.stem:
Your customer support agent handles account closure requests. The workflow requires: (1) verify customer identity via `get_customer`, (2) check for active subscriptions via `check_subscriptions`, (3) process closure via `close_account`. Production logs show the agent occasionally calls `close_account` before completing the identity verification step, resulting in unauthorized account closures.

A colleague suggests adding a system prompt instruction: "Always verify identity before closing accounts." What is the most effective approach?
options: The system prompt instruction is sufficient because it explicitly describes the required order.
Add a routing classifier that analyzes the request type and pre-selects the appropriate tools before the agent begins.
Implement a programmatic prerequisite that blocks `close_account` from executing until `get_customer` has returned a verified customer ID.
Add few-shot examples showing the correct three-step sequence alongside the system prompt instruction.
questions.explanation: When an ordering constraint has serious, irreversible consequences like unauthorized account closures, deterministic enforcement in code is the only reliable safeguard. The choice that blocks `close_account` from running until `get_customer` has returned a verified customer ID guarantees the prerequisite is met regardless of model behavior. Relying solely on a system-prompt instruction depends on probabilistic compliance, which the production logs already show fails; adding few-shot examples of the correct sequence only lowers the failure rate without eliminating it. A routing classifier that pre-selects tools addresses which tools to use, not the enforcement of their execution order, so it does not prevent the unsafe early call.

questions.task_desc: Multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff
questions.stem:
A customer contacts your support agent about three issues in a single message: a billing charge dispute, a missing order, and a request to update their email address. The agent processes these sequentially, taking 3-4 minutes per request. A senior architect suggests you redesign the handling approach.

What design change would most improve efficiency while maintaining accuracy?
options: Delegate all three issues to a single specialized "multi-issue" subagent that handles complex requests with multiple concerns.
Decompose the three concerns into distinct investigation items and process each in parallel using shared customer context, then compile a unified response.
Instruct the agent to address only the highest-priority issue per conversation turn and ask the customer to submit separate tickets for the remaining issues.
Process the three issues sequentially but cache intermediate results so subsequent issues benefit from data already retrieved.
questions.explanation: The three concerns (billing dispute, missing order, email update) are independent, so the serial 3-4 minute-per-issue processing is a pure latency bottleneck. Decomposing them into distinct investigation items that run in parallel over the already-retrieved shared customer context, then merging into one unified reply, slashes total latency while still investigating each issue fully. Asking the customer to resubmit the other issues as separate tickets shifts work onto them and worsens the experience; caching intermediate results between issues helps only marginally and leaves the serial dependency intact; and routing everything to one generic "multi-issue" subagent still handles the concerns sequentially and abandons the specialized coordinator/subagent decomposition that enables concurrency.

questions.task_desc: Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception
questions.stem:
Your customer support agent integrates with three backend MCP tools: a legacy billing system returning Unix timestamps, an order management system returning ISO 8601 dates, and a subscription service returning numeric status codes (1=active, 2=paused, 3=cancelled). The agent frequently misinterprets these heterogeneous formats when reasoning about customer records.

What is the most appropriate architectural fix?
options: Add format conversion instructions to the system prompt explaining how to interpret each tool's output conventions.
Update each backend system to return a uniform date and status format before the agent calls them.
Implement `PostToolUse` hooks that intercept tool results from each source and normalize timestamps, dates, and status codes into a consistent format before the model processes them.
Add a post-processing step after the agent produces its final response to re-format any dates and statuses that appear in the output.
questions.explanation: Registering PostToolUse hooks is the right architectural fix because these hooks fire after each tool returns and can deterministically transform the raw result, so the model always receives normalized timestamps, dates, and status codes regardless of backend heterogeneity. Modifying every backend to emit a uniform format may be infeasible for legacy systems and creates tight coupling, while describing each tool's conventions in the system prompt relies on probabilistic model compliance and wastes tokens. Reformatting the agent's final response happens too late—the model has already reasoned over inconsistent data, so any wrong conclusions it drew cannot be repaired after the fact.

questions.task_desc: Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception
questions.stem:
Your customer support agent has a policy: refunds above $500 require manager approval and must not be processed autonomously. Your system prompt states "Do not process refunds above $500 without manager approval." Production logs show this rule is violated in approximately 3% of cases.

What is the most effective way to guarantee compliance?
options: Strengthen the system prompt language: "You are strictly forbidden from processing refunds above $500 without explicit manager approval under any circumstances."
Add 10 few-shot examples in the system prompt, all demonstrating the agent requesting manager approval for high-value refunds.
Implement a validation step that runs after `process_refund` completes and reverses any refunds that exceeded the threshold.
Implement a hook that intercepts outgoing `process_refund` tool calls, checks the refund amount, and blocks execution or redirects to the manager approval workflow when the amount exceeds $500.
questions.explanation: Business rules requiring guaranteed compliance must be enforced programmatically rather than through prompt instructions alone. A hook that intercepts the `process_refund` tool call before it executes provides deterministic enforcement: it inspects the amount and blocks or redirects to manager approval regardless of how the model interprets its instructions, guaranteeing zero violations. Strengthening the prompt wording and adding few-shot approval examples only improve adherence probabilistically and cannot rule out the failing 3% of cases. Running a check that reverses refunds after `process_refund` completes is inadequate because the prohibited action has already occurred by the time it runs.

questions.task_desc: Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception
questions.stem:
A developer productivity agent has a `PostToolUse` hook that transforms raw Bash output. Currently the hook appends a formatted summary but also preserves the full raw output in the result passed to the model. Sessions exploring large codebases are hitting context limits faster than expected.

What change would most effectively reduce unnecessary context consumption from tool results?
options: Disable the `PostToolUse` hook entirely and let the model process raw Bash output directly.
Increase the model's `max_tokens` parameter to accommodate the additional context from both raw and formatted output.
Switch from `PostToolUse` hooks to pre-processing the Bash commands themselves to produce shorter output.
Modify the hook to return only the formatted summary to the model, trimming the verbose raw output rather than preserving it alongside the summary.
questions.explanation: A PostToolUse hook can rewrite the tool result before the model sees it (via the updatedToolOutput field), so having it return only the formatted summary and drop the redundant raw Bash output directly eliminates the duplicated context that is the root cause. Preserving both raw and summary output doubles the context spent per tool call, so trimming to just the summary is the effective fix. Disabling the hook entirely would sacrifice the useful normalization while still feeding verbose raw output; raising max_tokens only expands the model's output budget and does nothing to shrink the growing input context; and rewriting the underlying Bash commands is a far broader, often infeasible change that misses the simpler hook-level fix.

questions.stem:
You are building an automated code review system using Claude. The review must cover three aspects for every pull request: security vulnerabilities, style compliance, and performance implications. Each aspect has clear, defined criteria documented in your engineering handbook.

Which decomposition strategy is most appropriate?
options: A single comprehensive pass that examines all three aspects simultaneously to capture cross-cutting concerns.
Dynamic adaptive decomposition: have the agent start by scanning the full PR and generate a review plan based on what it finds.
Prompt chaining with sequential focused passes: one pass per review aspect (security, style, performance), each with dedicated criteria.
Spawn three fully independent agents without shared context, then merge their outputs in a final aggregation step.
questions.explanation: When a workflow decomposes into a fixed set of well-defined subtasks with documented criteria, prompt chaining into sequential focused passes (one per aspect) is the recommended pattern: each pass dedicates full attention and a tailored prompt to a single concern, trading a bit of latency for higher accuracy. The single comprehensive pass that examines all three aspects at once dilutes attention across concerns, which is exactly the problem chaining solves. Dynamic adaptive planning where the agent invents its own review plan adds unnecessary orchestration overhead for a structure that is already known in advance, and spawning three fully isolated agents with no shared context sacrifices cross-cutting integration signals for no benefit here.

questions.stem:
An engineering team asks you to design a Claude-based system to investigate a production incident. The system must root-cause a bug that was introduced somewhere in the past two weeks across an unknown set of files and services. The scope of the investigation cannot be known in advance.

Which decomposition approach is most appropriate?
options: Prompt chaining: design a fixed sequence of investigation steps (check logs, read configs, trace service calls) that the agent executes in order.
Have the agent run a comprehensive search of all changed files in the past two weeks and produce a ranked list of candidates for manual review.
Split the investigation between two agents: one agent reads logs while the other reads source code, and they report findings independently to a human operator.
Dynamic adaptive decomposition: the agent first maps the affected services and symptom timeline, then generates and prioritizes investigation subtasks based on what is discovered at each step.
questions.explanation: Open-ended investigations whose scope cannot be known in advance call for dynamic, adaptive decomposition, where the agent maps the affected services and symptom timeline and then generates and reprioritizes subtasks based on what each step reveals. A fixed, pre-planned sequence of investigation steps cannot adjust when the trail leads to unexpected service dependencies or anomalies, so it fits predictable multi-aspect reviews rather than exploratory root-cause work. Splitting the work across two agents that report independently to a human removes the coordinating intelligence needed to synthesize findings, and a single-pass brute-force scan of all recently changed files producing a ranked list is a static heuristic that never applies the model's iterative reasoning.

questions.stem:
A developer has been using Claude Code to investigate a legacy authentication service. After several hours, they have built up a detailed session with findings about the service's token validation logic. They need to step away and return tomorrow. Three core files in the authentication module will be modified overnight by another team.

What is the most reliable approach for resuming the investigation productively the next day?
options: Start a completely new session each time, since stale tool results from the previous session make resumption unreliable for any scenario.
Resume the named session with `--resume <session-name>` and notify the agent about the specific files that were changed, so it can re-analyze those files in the context of its prior understanding.
Resume the named session without any notification about file changes; the agent will detect modifications automatically when it next reads those files.
Use `fork_session` to create a branch of the current session before stepping away, then resume from the fork the next day.
questions.explanation: When resuming after code modifications, the reliable approach is to continue the named session with `--resume <session-name>` and explicitly tell the agent which files changed so it re-analyzes exactly those in light of its prior understanding. Always starting fresh is overly conservative — that is only warranted when prior tool results are broadly stale, not when a few known files changed. Resuming without notifying the agent fails because Claude Code does not automatically detect out-of-band file changes on resume. And forking is designed for exploring divergent approaches from a shared baseline, not for bridging a time gap or refreshing stale file content.

questions.stem:
You are leading a research investigation using Claude Code. You have completed an initial analysis of a competitor's public API documentation and want to explore two divergent architectural approaches for your response strategy: one focused on feature parity, one focused on differentiation. You want both explorations to start from the same analysis baseline.

Which session management approach best fits this scenario?
options: Use `--resume` to resume the current session twice in parallel, once for each exploration direction.
Run two separate new sessions, each with a copy of the analysis findings injected as context in the initial prompt.
Use `fork_session` to create two independent branches from the current session, then explore each approach in its respective branch.
Continue in the same session, exploring one approach, then using `/compact` to clear context before exploring the second approach.
questions.explanation: Forking is the purpose-built mechanism here: `fork_session` creates independent branches that each start from a copy of the shared analysis baseline and then diverge, so the feature-parity and differentiation explorations never contaminate each other while both inherit the same findings. Spinning up two fresh sessions with the findings pasted into the prompt duplicates context manually and discards the live session state. Resuming continues a single session sequentially rather than branching it into parallel divergent threads from a common point. Compacting to clear context before the second exploration is destructive and would lose the baseline needed to start the second branch cleanly.

questions.task_desc: Tool interface design with clear descriptions
questions.stem:
Your customer support agent has a `lookup_order` tool that is called at the right times, but frequently returns errors. Logs show the agent passes free-text descriptions like "order from last Tuesday" instead of the required ISO-8601 timestamp format. The tool description reads: "Retrieves order details given a date or identifier." No examples or format constraints are provided.

What change to the tool definition would most directly reduce these input format errors?
options: Split `lookup_order` into two tools: `lookup_order_by_id` and `lookup_order_by_date` to reduce ambiguity.
Add input format constraints, accepted value formats, and example inputs to the tool description so the model knows exactly how to format its calls.
Add a system prompt instruction: "Always use ISO-8601 format when calling lookup_order."
Add input schema validation that rejects malformed inputs and returns an error to the agent.
questions.explanation: Anthropic's tool-use guidance states that a detailed tool description is by far the most important factor in tool performance, and that it should spell out what each parameter means and how it must be formatted, supplemented with concrete example inputs for format-sensitive fields. Enriching the description with the required ISO-8601 format, accepted value formats, and examples gives the model the information it needs at the moment it builds the call, directly preventing the malformed inputs. A generic system-prompt instruction is more fragile and less targeted than tool-specific guidance, and schema validation that rejects bad inputs is reactive—it catches errors only after they occur (and a free-text date still passes a plain string type), producing wasteful error-retry cycles instead of preventing the mistake. Splitting the tool in two addresses selection ambiguity, but the stem says the tool is already being called at the right times, so disambiguation is not the problem.

questions.task_desc: Tool interface design with clear descriptions
questions.stem:
Your customer support agent has three tools with overlapping names and descriptions: `get_account_info` ("Get information about the account"), `fetch_account_details` ("Fetch account details"), and `retrieve_customer_record` ("Retrieve customer information"). All three call different backend systems with different data. The agent frequently picks the wrong tool.

Which approach most effectively resolves the misrouting?
options: Keep the current tools but randomize which one the agent calls, then merge the responses in a post-processing step.
Rename the tools to reflect their distinct data sources and rewrite their descriptions to explain what data each returns, what backend it queries, and what use case it serves.
Consolidate all three tools into one tool with a `source` parameter that specifies which backend to query.
Add a system prompt instruction listing all three tools and specifying exactly when each should be used based on the request type.
questions.explanation: The misrouting stems from the tool definitions themselves being indistinguishable, so the correct fix is at that layer: give each tool a distinct name and a description that spells out what data it returns, which backend it queries, and when to use it — the model routes primarily off tool names and descriptions. Adding a system-prompt guide on when to use each tool is a partial workaround that leaves the ambiguous definitions in place and is less robust. Collapsing all three into a single tool with a source parameter just relocates the same disambiguation problem into an enum argument while merging genuinely different data and use cases into one interface. Randomizing the call and merging responses is not a viable design.

questions.task_desc: Structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.stem:
Your customer support MCP tool `process_refund` returns the same error response for all failures: `{"status": "error", "message": "Operation failed"}`. The agent currently handles all errors by apologizing to the customer and ending the conversation. Logs show this response is triggered by: network timeouts, invalid refund amounts, refunds blocked by fraud detection, and expired order IDs.

What structured error response design would most improve the agent's ability to recover appropriately?
options: Return different HTTP status codes for each failure type and have the agent interpret the status code to determine recovery action.
Return a numeric error code and have the system prompt map each code to a recovery action.
Return a verbose error log with the full stack trace and system state so the agent has maximum information to reason from.
Return structured error metadata including `errorCategory` (transient/validation/permission/business), `isRetryable` boolean, and a human-readable explanation specific to the failure reason.
questions.explanation: Returning structured error metadata—an error category (transient/validation/permission/business), a retryable flag, and a failure-specific human-readable explanation—gives the agent exactly what it needs to pick the right recovery path: retry a transient timeout, ask the customer to correct a bad refund amount, or explain a fraud/policy block. Overloading HTTP status codes is weaker because those semantics don't map cleanly onto every failure class and are far less expressive than dedicated fields. Dumping a full stack trace and system state floods the context window without improving decision quality, and hiding recovery logic behind numeric codes mapped in the system prompt scatters the error contract into brittle, hard-to-maintain prompt text instead of the response itself.

questions.task_desc: Structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.stem:
A web search subagent in your research system calls `search_web` and receives the following response: `{"results": [], "status": "success"}`. The subagent reports to the coordinator: "Web search was successful but no relevant results were found." Later you discover the search API was actually down and returning empty results for all queries.

What change to the MCP tool's error handling would prevent this confusion?
options: Implement automatic retry in the MCP tool itself, so the agent never sees an empty result unless all retries were exhausted.
Add a `confidence` field to the response so the agent can infer whether the empty result is a real outcome or a failure.
Always return a non-empty results array by including fallback content when the actual search returns nothing.

Distinguish between access failures (where the backend could not be reached or returned an error) and valid empty results (where the search succeeded but found no matches), using the `isError` flag for the former.
questions.explanation: A backend/access failure and a genuinely empty result set are semantically different states that call for different agent behavior, so the tool must signal them distinctly. Marking backend failures with the MCP result's `isError` flag (or an equivalent error payload) while returning a normal success result only for truly empty searches gives the coordinator accurate information for retry and recovery decisions. Fabricating fallback content hides the outage and misleads the agent; a confidence score on an empty result is arbitrary and does not reliably encode "the API was down"; and retrying silently inside the tool still leaves the agent unable to tell a real empty result from a masked failure once retries are exhausted.

questions.task_desc: Structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.stem:
Your MCP tool `check_fraud_risk` returns `{"isError": true, "message": "Refund blocked"}` when a refund is flagged by fraud detection. The agent interprets this as a transient failure and retries the refund three times before giving up. Each retry generates a separate fraud alert in your compliance system.

What is the missing element in the error response design?
options: The response should include a `retry_after` timestamp so the agent knows when to retry.
The response should include the fraud risk score so the agent can decide whether the score is high enough to justify blocking.
The tool should suppress the `isError` flag for fraud blocks and instead return the result as a successful response with a `blocked: true` field.
The response should include `isRetryable: false` and a customer-appropriate explanation distinguishing this business rule block from a transient system error.
questions.explanation: The root problem is that a permanent business-rule block (a fraud decision) is indistinguishable from a transient system failure, so the agent wastes retries and floods compliance. Explicitly signaling non-retryability plus a customer-appropriate explanation tells the agent that retrying cannot resolve the block and how to communicate it, which is the correct fix. Adding a retry-after timestamp is wrong because it implies the block is temporary and encourages the very retries that should be avoided; hiding the error by returning a success with a "blocked" flag obscures the failure and complicates recovery logic; and returning the raw fraud risk score pushes an authorization decision onto the agent that it should not be making.

questions.task_desc: Tool distribution across agents and tool_choice
questions.stem:
You are building a multi-agent research system with a synthesis agent whose sole job is combining findings from subagents and producing a structured report. You have given the synthesis agent access to all 18 tools in your system: web search, file reading, database queries, email sending, calendar access, and more, reasoning that more tools provide more flexibility.

What problem does this configuration most likely introduce?
options: The additional tools will slow down the synthesis agent because the model must read all tool descriptions before producing output.
Having access to tools outside its specialization increases the likelihood the synthesis agent will misuse them, such as initiating new web searches instead of synthesizing the provided findings.
18 tools will exceed the context window limit for tool schemas, causing API errors on every request.
The synthesis agent will refuse to call any tools because it is overwhelmed by the number of choices.
questions.explanation: Overloading a narrowly-scoped agent with tools beyond its role degrades tool-selection reliability: a synthesis agent equipped with web search and other unrelated tools will sometimes take off-task actions (like launching fresh searches) instead of combining the findings it was given, which is the core risk here and matches Anthropic's multi-agent and tool-design guidance. The claim that the agent will refuse to call any tools overstates the effect—it still acts, just less reliably. The claim that 18 tool schemas exhaust the context window and error on every request is unrealistic for schemas of typical size, and the notion that reading tool descriptions is the main cause of slowdown mischaracterizes latency; the real problem is which tool gets chosen, not the time spent reading them.

questions.task_desc: Tool distribution across agents and tool_choice
questions.stem:
Your customer support system requires that every response from the `draft_response` agent includes a structured JSON summary before the agent returns its output. You want to guarantee the agent calls the `generate_summary` tool on every invocation, not optionally.

Which `tool_choice` configuration achieves this?
options: Remove all other tools from the agent's tool list so `generate_summary` is the only option available.
Set `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "generate_summary"}` to force the model to call `generate_summary` specifically.
Set `tool_choice: "auto"` so the model decides when the summary tool is needed.
Set `tool_choice: "any"` so the model must call at least one tool, though it may choose a different tool instead.
questions.explanation: Forcing a named tool via `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "generate_summary"}` guarantees the model invokes that exact tool on every call, which is what the requirement demands. Letting the model decide with `"auto"` permits a plain text response with no tool call at all, and `"any"` only guarantees that some tool is called, not that it is the summary tool. Stripping every other tool so only the summary tool remains would technically force it but removes legitimate tools the agent needs for its other work, making it a worse solution than the targeted forced-tool setting.

questions.task_desc: MCP server integration into Claude Code
questions.stem:
Your team is setting up a shared GitHub MCP server for all engineers on a project. The server requires a GitHub API token for authentication. You want every engineer who clones the repository to have the MCP server available without each person having to manually configure it, and you want to avoid committing the actual token to version control.

What is the correct configuration approach?
options: Add the MCP server to `~/.claude.json` on each developer's machine with their personal token hardcoded.
Add the MCP server to the project-scoped `.mcp.json` file with the token specified using environment variable expansion (e.g., `${GITHUB_TOKEN}`), and commit `.mcp.json` to the repository.
Add the MCP server configuration to the root `CLAUDE.md` file under a `[mcp_servers]` section.
Create a setup script that each developer runs once to add the MCP server to their personal `~/.claude.json` with their token.
questions.explanation: Committing a project-scoped `.mcp.json` at the repository root with the token supplied via environment variable expansion (e.g. `${GITHUB_TOKEN}`) is the documented pattern for shared team MCP servers: the file is checked into version control so every cloner gets the configuration automatically, while the secret is injected from each engineer's environment at runtime rather than stored in the repo. The two approaches that hardcode a personal token into `~/.claude.json` (whether edited by hand or via a one-time setup script) require manual per-person configuration and don't provide the automatic, version-controlled sharing the scenario needs. The suggestion to declare servers in a `[mcp_servers]` section of `CLAUDE.md` is invalid, because CLAUDE.md is a memory/instructions file and has no such MCP configuration mechanism.

questions.task_desc: MCP server integration into Claude Code
questions.stem:
A developer productivity agent frequently makes multiple exploratory tool calls to discover what data sources are available in your MCP server before it can answer user questions. This pattern increases latency and consumes context for routine requests.

What MCP feature most directly addresses this discovery overhead?
options: Add a caching layer that stores the results of previous exploratory calls and reuses them across sessions.
Add a `list_tools` meta-tool to the MCP server that returns all available tool names and descriptions.
Reduce the number of tools in the MCP server by merging similar tools together to minimize the discovery surface.
Expose content catalogs as MCP resources, giving the agent visibility into available data at connection time rather than through exploratory tool calls.
questions.explanation: MCP resources exist precisely to expose content catalogs and available data so an agent can see what is available at connection time (they can be enumerated by the host up front) rather than probing through repeated exploratory tool calls, which directly eliminates the discovery latency and context overhead described. Adding a discovery meta-tool that lists tools/resources still requires the agent to make a tool call to learn what exists, so it does not remove the round-trip. Merging tools only shrinks the surface while sacrificing functionality and still leaves discovery via tool calls, and a cross-session results cache treats the symptom rather than the cause and risks returning stale catalog data.

questions.task_desc: Built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob)
questions.stem:
A developer productivity agent needs to find all TypeScript files in a project that import from a deprecated module named `legacy-auth`. The project has thousands of files across many directories.

Which combination of built-in tools is most appropriate for this task?
options: Use `Read` on the project root directory to get a file listing, then recursively `Read` each subdirectory.
Use `Glob` to find all `.ts` files, then use `Read` to open each file and check whether it contains the import.
Use `Bash` to run `find . -name "*.ts"` and then `Bash` again to run `grep` on each file found.
Use `Grep` to search for the import pattern across all TypeScript files in the codebase.
questions.explanation: The dedicated content-search tool is purpose-built to scan file contents across a codebase and supports glob/type filtering (e.g., restricting to TypeScript files) plus regex patterns, so a single invocation finds every file importing the deprecated module efficiently. Shelling out to find plus per-file grep is discouraged because the harness provides dedicated tools that should be preferred over raw shell for this, and it is far slower. Enumerating files then opening each one to read its contents wastes many round-trips versus a direct content search, and using a file-reader for directory traversal is not how that tool works.

questions.task_desc: Built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob)
questions.stem:
A developer productivity agent needs to update a configuration value in a file. The value appears in a block of code that contains several nearly identical lines. When the agent uses `Edit`, the tool returns an error: "Match not unique: found 3 occurrences of the target text."

What is the correct fallback approach?
options: Use `Write` to overwrite the entire file with a corrected version, based on the contents loaded by `Read`.
Use `Bash` to run a `sed` command to replace all occurrences of the target text simultaneously.
Use `Grep` to locate the line number of each occurrence, then use `Edit` with a line number parameter to target the correct one.

Use `Read` to load the full file contents, identify the exact surrounding context needed to make the edit unique, and retry `Edit` with a larger `old_string` that uniquely identifies the correct location.
questions.explanation: The Edit tool does an exact string match and fails when the target text is not unique, so the intended fix is to re-read the file, capture enough surrounding lines to make `old_string` unambiguous, and retry Edit with that larger, unique anchor — surgical and low-risk. Running a shell `sed` replacement would change all three occurrences rather than the one intended value, and it bypasses the preferred built-in tools. Overwriting the whole file with Write is a heavier last resort that risks clobbering unrelated content and is only warranted if no unique anchor can be found. Locating occurrences by line number does not help because Edit has no line-number parameter; it matches on text only.

questions.stem: A senior engineer adds detailed coding standards and security guidelines to their `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` file. When a new team member joins and clones the repository, they report that Claude Code behaves differently and does not appear to follow the team's documented standards. What is the most likely cause?
options: The `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` file is user-scoped and not version-controlled, so teammates do not receive it when they clone the repository.
The new team member's Claude Code version is outdated and does not support shared configuration files.
CLAUDE.md files must be placed in the `.claude/` subdirectory to be recognized; a root-level `CLAUDE.md` is ignored.
The configuration hierarchy requires the project-level file to explicitly import from user-level files using `@import`.
questions.explanation: The home-directory `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is a user (personal) memory file that applies only to that individual across all their projects; it lives outside the repository and is never committed, so cloning the repo does not deliver it to teammates. To share standards, those instructions belong in the project-level `CLAUDE.md` (which can sit at the repo root or under `.claude/`) that is checked into version control. The choices blaming an outdated version or a required `@import` from user-level files describe mechanisms that do not exist, and the claim that a root-level `CLAUDE.md` is ignored is false since the repository root is a valid project memory location.

questions.stem: Your monorepo has a root `CLAUDE.md` that has grown to over 400 lines, covering Python conventions, TypeScript conventions, infrastructure rules, and testing standards. Developers report that Claude sometimes applies the wrong conventions to the wrong files, and the file is difficult to maintain. What is the best approach to reorganize this configuration?
options: Break the root `CLAUDE.md` into topic files and use `@import` directives in the root file to pull them all in unconditionally.
Split the content into multiple files in `.claude/rules/`, with each file covering a focused topic, and use path-scoped YAML frontmatter to activate rules only for relevant files.
Create a separate `CLAUDE.md` in each top-level package directory and delete the root file entirely.
Add inline section headers to the monolithic file and use the `/memory` command to tell Claude which section to prioritize for each task.
questions.explanation: Splitting the config into focused topic files under `.claude/rules/` and adding a `paths` glob to each file's YAML frontmatter is the intended mechanism here: a path-scoped rule loads only when Claude touches a matching file, which directly fixes both the wrong-conventions-on-wrong-files problem and the token/maintenance bloat. Putting a separate CLAUDE.md in every package and deleting the root forces duplication of shared rules and loses common context. Using headers plus the `/memory` command depends on manual per-session steering (and `/memory` is for browsing/editing memory, not prioritizing sections). Breaking the file into topic files that are pulled in unconditionally via `@import` improves readability but still loads every rule into context regardless of the file being edited, so it never solves the misapplied-conventions issue.

questions.stem: A developer wants a `/scaffold` skill that generates boilerplate for a new microservice. The skill runs many exploratory file reads and Bash commands to understand existing patterns before generating output, producing hundreds of lines of intermediate output. Teammates complain this pollutes their main conversation context. Which frontmatter setting resolves this?
options: Move the skill file from `.claude/skills/` to `.claude/commands/` so it runs as a command rather than a skill.
Add `argument-hint: "service-name"` to the frontmatter so the skill receives a clean input without inheriting session context.
Set `context: fork` in the skill's frontmatter to run the skill in an isolated sub-agent context that does not affect the main session.
Set `allowed-tools: []` in the skill's frontmatter to prevent tool use during execution.
questions.explanation: Setting `context: fork` runs the skill in a forked subagent, so all its exploratory reads, Bash calls, and intermediate output stay in that isolated context and only the final result returns to the main conversation — this directly resolves the context pollution. The `allowed-tools` choice does not help: that field grants tools permission without prompting and does not restrict availability, so setting it to an empty list neither disables tools nor isolates output. Relocating the file from the skills directory to the commands directory changes nothing, since custom commands and skills execute identically. Adding an `argument-hint` only provides an autocomplete hint for expected arguments and has no effect on context isolation.

questions.task_desc: Path-specific rules for conditional convention loading
questions.stem: Your team uses Terraform for infrastructure but only in the `infra/` directory. You want Claude to automatically apply Terraform naming conventions and module structure rules only when editing `.tf` files, without those rules appearing in unrelated Python or TypeScript sessions. What is the correct approach?
options: Create a rules file in `.claude/rules/terraform.md` with YAML frontmatter specifying `paths: ["infra/**/*"]` and listing the Terraform conventions.
Add the Terraform rules to the root `CLAUDE.md` under a clearly marked section, and instruct Claude in the system prompt to apply them only to `.tf` files.
Create a skill in `.claude/skills/terraform.md` with `allowed-tools` restricted to file operations on the `infra/` directory.
Create a `CLAUDE.md` file inside `infra/` that contains the Terraform rules so they apply only within that subdirectory.
questions.explanation: A path-scoped rule under `.claude/rules/` with a `paths:` YAML frontmatter glob (like `infra/**/*`) is the purpose-built mechanism for conditional convention loading: the rule enters context only when Claude works with matching files and stays out of unrelated Python or TypeScript sessions. A CLAUDE.md placed inside `infra/` does load only for files under that directory, but it is applied whenever files there are read and cannot glob-filter down to specific extensions, making it less precise than the path-scoped rule. Putting the rules in the root CLAUDE.md and asking Claude to self-restrict them by inference is unreliable and always consumes context, and a skill loads only on invocation or perceived relevance rather than firing automatically on file paths.

questions.stem: A developer asks Claude Code to add a single null-check to one function in a utility module. The function signature, expected behavior, and fix are all clear. Should they use plan mode or direct execution?
options: Use direct execution first, but run a quick plan mode scan afterward to catch any side effects before committing.
Direct execution, because the task is well-scoped with a clear fix that does not require architectural decisions or multi-file analysis.
Plan mode, because any code change could have unexpected side effects that need exploration before committing.
Plan mode, because exploring the codebase first prevents Claude from making assumptions about dependencies.
questions.explanation: Direct execution is the right call for a small, well-understood, single-location change like adding one null-check where the signature, behavior, and fix are already clear. Plan mode exists to explore and decide before acting on tasks that involve multiple valid approaches, architectural decisions, or multi-file changes—none of which apply here, so the answers invoking plan mode out of general caution about side effects or hidden dependencies over-apply it. The choice that runs direct execution first and then a plan-mode scan afterward inverts the workflow, since plan mode is a pre-execution planning gate rather than a post-execution audit.

questions.stem: A developer is using plan mode to explore a large codebase before deciding how to approach a library migration. During exploration, Claude generates dozens of tool results including full file reads, search outputs, and dependency traces. The developer notices the main conversation context is filling up rapidly and fears context exhaustion before the plan is complete. What is the most appropriate technique to preserve main session context during verbose exploration?
options: Run `/compact` immediately after each tool call to keep the context window from growing.
Use the Explore subagent to isolate verbose discovery output, having it return a structured summary to the main session rather than accumulating raw results.
Switch to direct execution partway through exploration so that Claude uses fewer tool calls.
Restrict Claude to reading only entry point files during plan mode to limit tool call volume.
questions.explanation: Delegating the verbose discovery to the read-only Explore subagent is correct because a subagent runs in its own isolated context window: its many tool calls and raw file reads stay inside the subagent, and only a concise structured summary is returned to the main session, preserving the parent's context budget. Abandoning plan mode for direct execution sacrifices the architectural exploration the developer actually needs. Compacting after every single tool call is operationally impractical and discards detail that may still be required, and restricting reads to only entry-point files artificially truncates the analysis and yields an incomplete migration plan.

questions.stem: A developer asks Claude Code to reformat date strings in a data pipeline. After the first attempt, the output format is partially correct but inconsistent: some dates are formatted as `YYYY-MM-DD`, others as `MM/DD/YYYY`. Prose instructions like "always use ISO 8601" have been tried twice without improvement. What technique is most likely to resolve the inconsistency?
options: Provide 2-3 concrete input/output examples showing the exact transformation expected, including edge cases like two-digit years and ambiguous formats.
Switch to plan mode so Claude can explore the codebase before formatting dates.
Rewrite the instruction to be more emphatic, using capitalization and repetition to signal importance.
Ask Claude to generate a formatting function first, then apply it in a second pass.
questions.explanation: When prose instructions are interpreted inconsistently, supplying a few concrete input/output examples (few-shot demonstrations) such as "3/15/2024" -> "2024-03-15" is the most reliable fix because it shows the exact transformation and edge-case handling unambiguously, whereas prose like "use ISO 8601" leaves room for interpretation. Simply making the instruction more emphatic with capitalization and repetition adds no new information and rarely resolves ambiguity. Switching to plan mode targets codebase exploration and architectural decisions rather than output-format precision, and asking Claude to first write a formatting function then apply it adds indirection without ever clarifying what the correct output should look like.

questions.stem: A development team is using Claude Code to implement a new authentication module. Before any implementation begins, the team lead wants to ensure Claude surfaces potential security considerations, edge cases, and design tradeoffs that the team may not have anticipated. Which iterative refinement technique is most appropriate here?
options: Provide a complete test suite first and ask Claude to write code that passes all tests.
Use the interview pattern: ask Claude to question the team about their requirements, constraints, and assumptions before proposing a design.
Write a detailed specification document and pass it to Claude for direct implementation.
Start with a minimal implementation and iterate by describing issues found during code review.
questions.explanation: The interview pattern — having Claude interrogate the team about requirements, constraints, and assumptions before proposing any design — is purpose-built to surface security considerations, edge cases, and tradeoffs the team has not yet anticipated, which is exactly the stated goal and especially valuable in high-risk domains like authentication. Handing over a finished spec for direct implementation locks in the team's existing blind spots and skips the discovery step entirely. Starting from a minimal implementation and refining through code review defers issue discovery to after code exists, when changes are costlier, and providing a complete test suite up front only validates behavior the team already thought to specify, so it cannot reveal considerations they never imagined.

questions.stem: Claude Code generated a data transformation function that has three separate issues: an off-by-one error in a loop, a missing null check for an optional field, and an incorrect sort order.
