Each issue is in a completely different part of the function and none of them interact. What is the recommended approach for addressing these issues?
options: Fix them sequentially: address each issue in a separate message and verify the fix before moving to the next.
Ask Claude to regenerate the entire function from scratch rather than patching the existing code.
Report all three issues in a single detailed message so Claude has full context for all fixes at once.
Address the off-by-one error and null check together since they are in loops, then fix the sort order separately.
questions.explanation: When the defects are genuinely independent — as this stem stresses (different parts of the function, no interaction) — sequential iteration is the recommended approach: fix each issue separately and verify it before moving on, which reduces the chance of one fix interfering with another and keeps each change easy to review. Reporting everything in a single detailed message is the technique reserved for issues that interact and must be resolved together with full context, which is not the case here. Regenerating the whole function from scratch needlessly discards working code, and pairing the loop error with the null check is an arbitrary grouping since the stem says none of the issues interact.

questions.stem: A CI pipeline runs Claude Code to generate test cases for each pull request. The team discovers that Claude is consistently suggesting test scenarios that already exist in the existing test files, wasting review time. Which approach in the CLAUDE.md configuration most directly addresses this problem?
options: Document in `CLAUDE.md` the testing standards, available fixtures, and instruct Claude to review existing test files before suggesting new scenarios.
Use `--output-format json` with `--json-schema` so that test suggestions are machine-parseable for deduplication.
Add the `-p` flag to the CI invocation command to prevent interactive prompts.
Add a post-processing script to the pipeline that compares Claude's suggestions against the existing test files and filters duplicates.
questions.explanation: The root cause is that Claude lacks awareness of the tests that already exist, so the most direct fix is to document testing standards and fixtures in CLAUDE.md and instruct Claude to review existing test files before proposing new scenarios—this prevents duplicates at generation time. Adding a non-interactive flag only avoids CI prompt hangs and does nothing to change the content of suggestions. Emitting machine-parseable JSON output improves parsing but still produces the same duplicate suggestions, and a downstream deduplication script is a workaround that adds pipeline complexity without addressing why Claude generates duplicates in the first place.

questions.stem: A developer has identified three issues in a data processing module: two logic bugs that interact because they both affect the same intermediate result, and a set of five inconsistent variable names scattered across multiple files. The logic bugs produce incorrect output only when both are present; the naming violations are independent style issues. How should these issues be addressed using iterative refinement?
options: Report all eight issues in a single message so Claude can resolve them together with full context.
Fix all naming violations first since they are simpler, then tackle the two logic bugs in a single message.
Fix each of the eight issues in eight separate sequential messages, verifying each before proceeding.
Send the two interacting logic bugs in one message so Claude can resolve them together, then address the naming violations sequentially in separate follow-up messages after verifying the bug fixes.
questions.explanation: Batching in iterative refinement is driven by interdependence, not count or complexity. The two logic bugs interact through the same intermediate result and only fail together, so Claude needs both in one message to reason about the shared context and fix them coherently; the five naming issues are independent style changes, so iterating on them one at a time with verification after each is the right approach. Lumping all issues into a single message adds noise while the interacting bugs are being resolved, ordering by simplicity ignores the interaction that actually dictates batching, and splitting the two interacting bugs into separate sequential messages risks an incomplete fix because neither can be correctly resolved without the other.

questions.stem: A team is adding a caching layer to an existing microservice. The service was written two years ago by engineers who are no longer on the team, and its internal patterns for dependency injection and lifecycle management are unfamiliar to the current developer. Before writing any code, the developer wants to ensure Claude surfaces all relevant design considerations. Which iterative refinement technique is most appropriate?
options: Ask Claude to implement a generic Redis-based caching layer using standard patterns for the framework, then review its output for compatibility issues.
Provide the service's entry point files to Claude and ask it to generate a caching design document for review.
Use the interview pattern: ask Claude to question you about the service's architecture, existing lifecycle hooks, and caching requirements before proposing any design.
Start with a minimal proof-of-concept cache for one endpoint and iterate by describing any failures encountered during testing.
questions.explanation: The interview pattern, where Claude questions the developer about the service's architecture, lifecycle hooks, and caching requirements before proposing anything, is designed exactly for cases where the developer may not know which considerations matter. Because the service is unfamiliar legacy code, this interactive questioning surfaces assumptions about lifecycle management, cache invalidation, and dependency injection that the developer wouldn't know to specify upfront. Immediately implementing a generic Redis layer risks patterns incompatible with the unknown service; handing over entry-point files for a one-shot design document uses real context but cannot interactively draw out considerations the developer hasn't thought of; and building a proof-of-concept then iterating on test failures defers discovery to runtime instead of surfacing design issues before any code is written.

questions.stem: A CI pipeline uses Claude Code to review pull requests and needs to post inline comments on specific lines in GitHub. The pipeline currently receives Claude's output as unstructured text, which requires a fragile regex parser to extract file paths, line numbers, and comment text. The parser breaks regularly as Claude's output format drifts between runs. What is the most robust solution?
options: Add stricter formatting instructions to the system prompt specifying the exact text structure expected, and add validation to reject any response that does not match.
Add a post-processing step that uses a second Claude call to normalize the first response into a consistent structured format.
Use `--output-format json` combined with `--json-schema` to define a schema with `file`, `line`, and `comment` fields, so each finding is machine-parseable by construction.
Switch from inline comments to a single summary comment, which is easier to extract since it does not require line-level parsing.
questions.explanation: The most robust fix is to have Claude Code emit schema-conformant JSON via its `--output-format json` and `--json-schema` flags, defining `file`, `line`, and `comment` fields; the output is machine-parseable by construction, so format drift and the fragile regex layer are eliminated entirely. Tightening prompt formatting instructions with validation still depends on Claude voluntarily maintaining a consistent text format across runs, which is the exact root cause of the breakage. Adding a second Claude call to normalize the output introduces extra latency, cost, and another non-deterministic step, while switching to a single summary comment abandons the required inline line-level feature rather than solving the parsing problem.

questions.stem: You maintain a monorepo with five packages: a Python backend, a TypeScript frontend, a Go service, shared infrastructure Terraform, and a documentation site. Each package has different linting standards, testing conventions, and framework-specific rules. The root `CLAUDE.md` has grown to 600 lines and developers report that rules intended for one package often bleed into sessions working on another. What is the most modular and maintainable solution using CLAUDE.md configuration?
options: Move all rules into `.claude/rules/` files and tag each with a `projects:` key in their frontmatter specifying which subdirectory the rule applies to.
Delete the root `CLAUDE.md` and rely entirely on package-level files, accepting that shared conventions must be duplicated across packages.
Keep the 600-line root file but add explicit section headers and instruct developers to tell Claude which section applies at the start of each session.
Create a `CLAUDE.md` in each package directory with that package's rules, and use `@import` in the root `CLAUDE.md` to include shared conventions that apply to all packages.
questions.explanation: Placing a CLAUDE.md in each package directory scopes that package's rules to sessions working in that directory (nested files load on demand when Claude touches files there), while the root file uses `@import` to pull in conventions shared across all packages — this is the officially documented modular pattern and it eliminates cross-package rule bleed without duplication. Keeping the single 600-line file and asking developers to announce the relevant section each session relies on manual discipline that is error-prone and does not scale. Deleting the root file forces the same shared conventions to be duplicated across every package, causing maintenance drift. The `.claude/rules/` approach is real, but it scopes rules with a `paths:` glob frontmatter field, not a `projects:` subdirectory key as described, and does not provide the package-level CLAUDE.md isolation the scenario calls for.

questions.task_desc: Explicit criteria for precision and false positive reduction
questions.stem: A CI code review prompt flags 60% of pull requests for "potential security vulnerabilities." Developers have stopped reading the reports because nearly all flags are false positives on standard input validation patterns they consider acceptable. Adding "only report high-confidence findings" to the prompt has had no measurable effect. What is the most effective next step?
options: Temporarily disable the security category and add explicit criteria defining which patterns constitute a reportable vulnerability versus acceptable practice, with concrete code examples for each.
Add a post-processing confidence threshold: discard any finding where Claude rates its own confidence below 80%.
Increase the review model to a larger tier to improve its judgment on security issues.
Replace the security review prompt with a rule-based static analysis tool that has zero false positives.
questions.explanation: Vague instructions like "only report high-confidence findings" fail because they never define what actually counts as a reportable vulnerability, so the root cause is imprecise criteria, not model capability. The winning approach pauses the noisy category to restore developer trust while supplying explicit, example-backed criteria that let the model distinguish real issues from the acceptable validation patterns the team already tolerates. Swapping in a "zero false positive" static analyzer is both technically unrealistic and throws away the AI review instead of fixing it; simply using a larger model does not address undefined criteria; and gating on the model's self-reported confidence relies on scores that LLMs calibrate poorly and that just re-express the same vague judgment.

questions.task_desc: Explicit criteria for precision and false positive reduction
questions.stem: A Claude-based pull request reviewer raises "magic number" warnings on every numeric literal in the codebase, including well-understood constants like HTTP status codes and standard buffer sizes. Developers want magic numbers flagged only when they appear in business logic with no explanation. How should the prompt be updated?
options: Add an instruction to "use good judgment about whether numbers are truly magic numbers."
Provide a list of allowed numeric values that should never be flagged.
Add a severity field to findings and instruct Claude to omit any finding with severity "low."

Define explicit criteria: flag numeric literals that appear in business logic calculations with no accompanying comment or named constant; exclude HTTP status codes, standard buffer sizes, and any value documented in a comment.
questions.explanation: Giving Claude explicit, specific criteria — a clear rule for what counts as a magic number (unexplained numeric literals in business-logic calculations) plus an explicit exclusion set (status codes, standard buffer sizes, commented values) — turns a fuzzy judgment call into a concrete decision rule, which is the most reliable way to fix over-flagging. Telling Claude to "use good judgment" is the same vagueness that produced the problem, so it won't add consistency. Adding a severity field and dropping low-severity findings never defines what "magic" means and can suppress legitimate flags, and maintaining a hardcoded allowlist of permitted numbers is brittle and fails to generalize to new constants.

questions.task_desc: Explicit criteria for precision and false positive reduction
questions.stem: A Claude-based code reviewer is generating inconsistent feedback on documentation quality: sometimes flagging missing docstrings for private helper functions, sometimes not; sometimes requiring full parameter descriptions, sometimes accepting one-line summaries. The team wants consistent, predictable documentation feedback on public API functions only. Which change most directly addresses the inconsistency?
options: Define explicit documentation criteria: public API functions must have a docstring with a one-sentence summary, parameter descriptions, and return value description; private functions are excluded from documentation checks.
Run three parallel review instances and accept feedback that appears in at least two of the three.
Instruct Claude to "apply documentation standards consistently and carefully."
Restrict the review prompt to only one concern at a time, alternating between security, documentation, and style in separate runs.
questions.explanation: The inconsistency comes from an undefined standard: without an explicit specification of what must be documented, for which functions, and at what level of detail, Claude must re-infer the threshold on each run and produces variable results. Defining precise, scoped criteria (public API functions require a docstring with a summary, parameter descriptions, and a return description; private functions are excluded) removes that ambiguity and is the most direct fix. Simply telling Claude to "apply standards consistently" restates the vague instruction already causing the problem; running multiple instances and taking a majority vote adds cost and can suppress valid feedback that surfaces in only one run without fixing the definition; and splitting reviews into one concern per run separates concerns but still leaves "adequate documentation" undefined.

questions.task_desc: Few-shot prompting for consistency and quality
questions.stem: A team uses Claude to generate code review comments for pull requests. The comments are technically accurate but vary widely in format: some include file paths and line numbers, others are vague summaries, some use bullet points, and others write prose paragraphs. Developers want a consistent, actionable format: file path, line number, issue description, suggested fix. What is the most effective way to achieve this?
options: Add a format specification to the system prompt listing the required fields.
Ask Claude to self-review its output and reformat any comment that does not match the required structure.
Use `--output-format json` and parse the results in a post-processing step to normalize format.
Provide 2-4 few-shot examples in the prompt showing the exact desired output format for different types of issues, including file path, line number, issue description, and suggested fix.
questions.explanation: When technically-correct output varies widely in format, providing a few concrete few-shot examples that demonstrate the exact desired structure (file path, line number, issue description, suggested fix) is the most reliable way to make the requirement unambiguous and enforce consistency. Simply listing the required fields as a format specification in the system prompt is the approach the stem implies has already failed, since the comments are still inconsistent. Switching the CLI to JSON output and post-processing only changes the response envelope, not the semantic quality or completeness of the generated content, and adds parsing complexity; asking the model to self-review and reformat adds a fragile extra round-trip that can still start from inconsistent intermediate output.

questions.task_desc: Few-shot prompting for consistency and quality
questions.stem: A structured data extraction system is extracting contract clauses from legal documents. The model handles standard clauses well but consistently misclassifies ambiguous clauses that could fit two or more categories, such as a clause that contains both termination conditions and force majeure language. What few-shot prompting approach is most effective for improving accuracy on these edge cases?
options: Add a confidence score field and route all low-confidence classifications to human review without attempting to improve the model's judgment.
Switch from classification to extraction: ask Claude to extract the clause text without categorizing it, then apply a rule-based classifier.

Create targeted few-shot examples that specifically demonstrate ambiguous-case handling: showing the reasoning for why a clause with both termination and force majeure language belongs to one category and not the other.
Add more few-shot examples of standard, unambiguous clauses to reinforce the classification schema overall.
questions.explanation: The most effective approach is to add targeted few-shot examples that specifically demonstrate the ambiguous cases and show the reasoning for why a boundary-spanning clause belongs to one category rather than another; this teaches the model the exact discrimination logic it is currently getting wrong. Adding more standard, unambiguous examples only reinforces behavior that already works and does not address the edge-case gap. Switching to text extraction plus a rule-based classifier sidesteps the classification challenge rather than solving it, and rule-based systems tend to handle genuine ambiguity poorly. Adding a confidence score with routing to human review is a reasonable operational safeguard but does nothing to improve the model's own accuracy, which is what the question asks for.

questions.task_desc: Few-shot prompting for consistency and quality
questions.stem: A document extraction system uses Claude to pull financial figures from quarterly earnings reports. The reports have varied formats: some use tables, some use inline prose, some use both. Extraction accuracy for tabular data is high (94%) but prose-only documents show only 72% accuracy. What is the most targeted approach to improve prose extraction accuracy?
options: Use a separate prompt for prose documents that instructs Claude to first identify all sentences containing numbers, then extract figures from those sentences only.
Pre-process all documents to convert prose financial data into table format before sending to Claude.
Add few-shot examples specifically showing correct extraction from prose-formatted documents, including cases where numbers appear in sentence form rather than tables.
Increase the system prompt's emphasis on accuracy with stronger language and more detailed instructions.
questions.explanation: The gap is format-specific: the model already extracts tables well (94%) but struggles with prose (72%), so the most targeted fix is adding few-shot examples that demonstrate correct extraction from prose where figures appear in sentence form. Merely strengthening the wording and adding emphasis in the system prompt does not teach the model the prose pattern it is missing. Pre-processing prose into tables just shifts the hard extraction problem to an unreliable upstream step, and a separate multi-step prompt that first isolates number-containing sentences adds complexity while risking missed figures that are discussed without landing in an isolated sentence.

questions.task_desc: Structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
questions.stem: Your extraction pipeline uses a prompt that requests JSON output with a code block. In production, approximately 3% of responses have JSON syntax errors: missing commas, unescaped characters, or truncated objects. These errors break your downstream parser and require manual reprocessing. What is the most reliable way to eliminate JSON syntax errors?
options: Use a regex post-processor to fix the most common syntax errors before passing output to the parser.
Switch to tool use with a defined JSON schema as the input parameter; extract structured data from the `tool_use` response block.
Add a validation step that re-runs the prompt if the output fails JSON parsing, up to 3 retries.
Add an instruction to the prompt: "Your response must be valid JSON. Double-check for syntax errors before responding."
questions.explanation: Defining a tool with a JSON input schema and reading the structured data from the tool_use block is the most reliable approach: the model populates the schema-defined input parameters directly instead of hand-writing free-text JSON, so malformed-JSON failures like missing commas, unescaped characters, or truncation are eliminated by construction. Re-running the prompt on parse failure only lowers the failure rate rather than removing it, and each retry adds latency and cost. Adding a "produce valid JSON and self-check" instruction depends on unreliable model self-correction, and a regex post-processor is a brittle patch that cannot cover every syntax-error pattern.

questions.task_desc: Structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
questions.stem: Your structured data extraction pipeline processes invoices. You have two extraction tools: `extract_invoice_schema` and `extract_receipt_schema`. For each document, you do not know in advance which type it is. After switching to `tool_choice: "auto"`, you observe that 30% of the time the model returns a text description of what it found rather than calling either tool. What is the correct fix?
options: Set `tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_invoice_schema"}` to always call a specific tool.
Add a system prompt instruction: "Always call one of the extraction tools and never return text."
Set `tool_choice: "any"` to guarantee the model calls one of the available extraction tools without specifying which one.
Merge both schemas into a single `extract_document_schema` tool with an optional `document_type` field.
questions.explanation: Setting tool_choice to "any" forces the model to call exactly one of the available tools without dictating which, which is the documented way to eliminate free-text responses while still letting the model pick between the invoice and receipt extractors. Forcing a single named tool would misclassify every document you don't yet know the type of. Adding a system-prompt instruction is only probabilistic steering and is precisely the approach that already failed with a 30% text-response rate. Merging both schemas into one tool is a valid but heavier re-architecture when a single configuration value solves the problem.

questions.stem: An invoice extraction pipeline uses tool use with a strict JSON schema. After validation, 8% of extracted invoices fail a business rule check: the sum of line item amounts does not equal the `total_amount` field. These are semantic errors, not schema syntax errors. The invoices are well-formed documents with no missing data. How should you implement the retry loop?
options: Retry up to 3 times with the same prompt; schema-compliant extraction will converge on a valid answer with additional attempts.
Flag all invoices with this error as missing data and route them directly to human review without retry.
On failure, append the original document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation error ("line items sum to X but total_amount is Y") to the follow-up prompt for model self-correction.
Add a `calculated_total` field to the schema and populate it with the sum of line items in post-processing, then use the calculated total as the canonical value.
questions.explanation: Effective retry loops feed the model the specific discrepancy so it can self-correct; giving it the source document, its own failed extraction, and the exact mismatch ("line items sum to X but total_amount is Y") supplies everything needed to fix the arithmetic on the next attempt, especially since the documents are well-formed with no missing data. Simply retrying with the identical prompt gives no new signal, so the model tends to reproduce the same error rather than converge. Treating the failures as missing data and sending them straight to human review is premature and mislabels a semantic mismatch as absent data, wasting an inexpensive automated fix. Computing the total from line items in post-processing and overwriting the extracted total silently hides the error and yields a wrong total whenever a line item itself was misread.

questions.stem: A CI code review pipeline is generating false positive findings at a high rate. The team wants to understand which specific code constructs are being flagged incorrectly so they can refine the prompt. The current findings output only includes `file`, `line`, and `description`. What schema change would best enable systematic false positive analysis?
options: Add an `is_false_positive` boolean field and instruct Claude to self-label its own false positives.
Add a `category` field so findings can be grouped by issue type for aggregate analysis.
Add a `confidence` field (0-100) to each finding so the team can filter by confidence threshold.
Add a `detected_pattern` field to each finding that records the specific code construct or pattern that triggered the finding.
questions.explanation: Because the team specifically wants to know which code constructs are being flagged incorrectly, the most useful schema addition is a field capturing the specific pattern or construct that triggered each finding; that lets them tally which constructs generate the most false positives and target the prompt accordingly. A numeric confidence score only supports threshold filtering and never reveals what was actually flagged, and a coarse issue-type category enables aggregate grouping but not construct-level debugging. Asking the model to self-label its own findings as false positives is unreliable, since without ground truth it cannot distinguish true from false positives (and would presumably not emit a finding it believed was false).

questions.stem: A compliance team runs a weekly audit that analyzes 50,000 contract documents for regulatory clauses. The audit results are reviewed by a compliance analyst every Monday morning. The team is considering the Message Batches API. A colleague raises a concern: "What if a batch fails partway through the 50,000 documents?" How should partial batch failures be handled?
options: Switch to real-time API calls with retry logic; the batch API is not suitable for mission-critical compliance workloads.
Set a shorter processing window timeout to force faster completion and reduce the risk of partial failures.
Use the `custom_id` field to correlate each request with its response, identify which documents returned error responses, and resubmit only those documents in a new batch.
Always resubmit the entire batch; tracking partial failures adds implementation complexity.
questions.explanation: In the Message Batches API each request is processed independently and ends with one of four result types (succeeded, errored, canceled, expired), and the required `custom_id` field maps every result back to its originating request. The correct approach is therefore to use `custom_id` to pinpoint the requests that errored or expired and resubmit only those, preserving the batch API's 50% cost savings and higher throughput. Reprocessing the entire batch needlessly re-runs successful documents and wastes cost; there is no way to shorten the processing window on demand since batches run asynchronously (managed by Anthropic, up to a 24-hour expiration); and switching to real-time calls is unnecessary because this weekly, latency-tolerant compliance audit is a textbook fit for asynchronous batch processing.

questions.stem: A developer generates a 300-line module using Claude Code in one session, then asks the same session to review the code for bugs. The review returns "looks good" with minor style suggestions but misses two logic errors that a colleague catches in manual review. What is the most likely cause, and what architectural change addresses it?
options: The review prompt was too vague; adding more explicit review criteria to the same session would catch the missed errors.
The model tier used for generation is more capable than the one used for review; switching both to the same tier resolves the quality gap.

The reviewing instance retains reasoning context from generation, making it less likely to question its own decisions. Use a second independent Claude instance without the generation context to perform the review.
The context window was too large; the fix is to limit code generation to smaller chunks so the review has less to process.
questions.explanation: When the same session both writes and reviews code, it carries forward the reasoning and assumptions that produced the code, so it tends to rationalize rather than challenge its own decisions; spinning up a fresh, independent instance with no generation context lets it read the code without those assumptions and catch logic errors the author missed. Blaming context-window size and generating smaller chunks confuses a length problem with a bias problem. Adding more explicit review criteria in the same session may help some issue categories but leaves the self-review bias intact. Attributing the gap to a model-tier mismatch is unfounded here, since a single session used one model for both tasks; the fix is structural independence, not tier alignment.

questions.stem: A financial document extraction pipeline is failing on the "guarantor address" field for a set of loan summaries. After 3 retry attempts with error feedback, the field still extracts as null. Each retry prompt includes the original document and the validation error. What should you investigate before scheduling further retries?
options: Check whether the guarantor address is actually present in the loan summary document, or whether the document only references it by directing the reader to an external exhibit.
Switch to a larger model tier for the retry attempts, since the current model may lack the reasoning capacity for this field.
Increase the retry limit from 3 to 5, since complex field extraction may require more attempts to converge.
Restructure the schema to make the guarantor address field optional, allowing the pipeline to proceed when the field cannot be extracted.
questions.explanation: Retry-with-feedback loops only converge when the information needed to satisfy the validation actually exists in the input; if the loan summary merely points the reader to an external exhibit rather than stating the guarantor address, no amount of retrying can extract data that is absent, so verifying the field's presence in the source is the correct first diagnostic step. Simply raising the retry ceiling piles on more attempts without diagnosing why they fail, and upgrading to a larger model tier may help genuinely hard extractions but cannot conjure missing data. Making the field optional merely suppresses the validation error instead of addressing the root cause.

questions.stem: A developer builds a code generation pipeline where Claude generates a 200-line module, and then the same session reviews the generated code for correctness. The review returns only minor style suggestions and misses a logic error in the error handling path. A second developer reviewing the code manually finds the bug immediately. What architectural change would make the review phase more effective?
options: Switch to a larger model tier for the review step to improve reasoning quality.

Use a second independent Claude instance without the generation context to perform the review, since the generating instance retains reasoning context that makes it less likely to question its own decisions.
Add more explicit review criteria to the generation session's system prompt to help it catch logic errors.
Run the review immediately after generation before any other tool calls accumulate in the context window.
questions.explanation: The root problem is structural: a model reviewing code it just produced carries over its own reasoning and assumptions, so it tends to validate rather than challenge its output and misses errors a fresh reviewer catches. Spinning up a separate, independent instance that reviews the code with no generation context removes that self-review bias, which is the documented rationale for multi-instance review architectures. Enriching the generating session's system prompt with more review criteria, or running the review before other context accumulates, still leaves the same instance reviewing its own work; upgrading to a larger model raises general capability but does not eliminate the self-review bias.

questions.stem: A customer support agent is handling a complex billing dispute that spans 20+ turns. A customer mentioned early in the conversation that they were charged $847.50 on March 3rd for a service they cancelled on February 28th. Midway through the conversation, the agent references the charge as "the overcharge from last month" without the specific amount. The customer disputes the agent's understanding of the case. What context management technique would prevent this?
options: Use `/compact` at the start of each conversation to summarize prior turns into a shorter representation.
Instruct the agent to re-read the full conversation history before each response.
Extract transactional facts (amounts, dates, order numbers, statuses) into a persistent "case facts" block that is included at the beginning of every subsequent prompt in the conversation.
Increase the model's context window by switching to a larger tier to retain the full conversation without summarization.
questions.explanation: Pinning precise transactional facts (amounts, dates, order numbers, statuses) into a persistent "case facts" block keeps exact values verbatim in every prompt, so the model cannot drift into vague references like "the overcharge from last month." Compacting or summarizing prior turns manages context length but actively risks condensing those specific numbers and dates into imprecise paraphrases—the very failure described. Simply switching to a larger context window still lets the model paraphrase and does nothing to guarantee fidelity, and instructing the agent to re-read the entire history each turn is impractical over 20+ turns and offers no guarantee the exact figures survive.

questions.stem: A multi-agent research pipeline aggregates findings from six subagents, each returning 800-1,200 tokens of raw tool output and reasoning. The synthesis agent receives all results in a single large message. The final report consistently omits or contradicts findings that appeared in the middle sections of the aggregated input. What is the most likely cause and the most effective mitigation?
options: The subagents are returning conflicting information; add a deduplication step before synthesis.
The synthesis agent's context window is exhausted; route some subagent outputs through a secondary summarization agent before passing to synthesis.

The "lost in the middle" effect causes models to reliably process content at the beginning and end of long inputs but miss middle sections. Mitigate by placing key findings summaries at the beginning of aggregated inputs and organizing sections with explicit headers.
The synthesis agent is hitting its output token limit; increase `max_tokens` to allow a longer response.
questions.explanation: The symptom—content is lost specifically from the middle of a long aggregated input while the beginning and end are handled well—is the classic signature of the "lost in the middle" positional-attention effect, so the fix is to front-load key findings and use explicit section headers to make the material easier to attend to. The deduplication idea targets conflicting data, not positional loss; raising max_tokens only affects output length, not how input is attended to. The context-exhaustion/secondary-summarization option is unlikely here because roughly six responses of about 1,000 tokens each is far below a modern context window, and adding a summarization stage does not address the positional attention problem.

questions.stem: A customer opens a support chat saying "I'd like to speak to a human please. I've been dealing with this billing issue for three weeks and I'm frustrated." The agent has already identified this as a standard billing adjustment that it can resolve in 2 steps using its available tools. Your agent responds by saying "I can help with that right away, let me pull up your account." and proceeds to investigate. The customer repeats their request for a human. What is wrong with this behavior and what should be corrected?
options: The agent should complete its investigation, present the proposed solution, and escalate only if the customer still insists afterward.
The agent should apologize for the wait, then proceed with automated resolution since the case is within its capability.
The agent should honor an explicit customer request for a human agent immediately on first request, without attempting investigation first.
The agent should detect negative sentiment and escalate once the frustration score exceeds a defined threshold.
questions.explanation: An explicit request to speak with a human is a hard escalation signal that must be honored immediately on first request, regardless of whether the agent could technically resolve the issue itself. The choice to keep investigating and only escalate if the customer insists afterward directly ignores the explicit request and compounds the frustration (this is essentially the agent's current wrong behavior). Apologizing and then proceeding with automated resolution makes the same error, treating the agent's capability rather than the customer's stated preference as the deciding factor. Waiting for a sentiment or frustration score to cross a threshold is also wrong, because sentiment is only a proxy and is moot once the customer has plainly asked for a human.

questions.stem: Your web search subagent encounters a timeout while fetching results for a competitor analysis. The engineering team implements a change so the subagent catches the timeout internally and returns an empty result set with `status: "success"` and `results: []`. The coordinator receives this and the synthesis agent produces a report that is missing the competitive section entirely, with no indication anything went wrong. What is the problem with this approach?
options: The synthesis agent should be responsible for detecting empty sections and re-triggering the search subagent directly.
The coordinator should always validate that each subagent returned non-empty results before proceeding to synthesis.
The timeout threshold should be increased so that the subagent does not time out before returning real results.
Silently returning an empty success result prevents the coordinator from making any recovery decision and allows incomplete work to pass as complete output.
questions.explanation: The core failure is that the subagent disguises an error as success by reporting an empty result set with a success status, which strips the coordinator of any ability to retry, route around the gap, or annotate the report with a coverage warning—so incomplete work silently passes as complete. Making the synthesis agent detect empty sections and re-run the search misplaces recovery responsibility onto a component that lacks the tools and context to fetch data. Requiring the coordinator to always reject empty results is both a downstream band-aid that ignores the real cause (the subagent misreporting failure) and over-broad, since legitimately empty results can occur. Raising the timeout threshold only treats the symptom and does nothing about the flawed practice of masking errors as successes.

questions.task_desc: Context management in large codebase exploration
questions.stem: An agent is performing a deep exploration of a 200,000-line legacy codebase to understand its payment processing flow. After approximately 90 minutes and dozens of file reads, the agent begins referencing "standard patterns in payment systems" rather than the specific classes and flows it found earlier, and its answers become inconsistent with findings from the first hour. What is the most likely cause and the best mitigation strategy?
options: Context degradation: as the session grows, specific findings from early in the session compete with general knowledge and the agent's position-attention effects. Mitigate by having the agent maintain a scratchpad file recording key findings throughout the session.
The agent is using the wrong tools; switching from `Read` to `Grep` for file exploration would reduce context consumption.
The agent has reached a rate limit; the fix is to add delays between tool calls to stay within limits.
The legacy codebase's file structure is too complex for a single agent; break it into independent subsystems and run separate agents on each subsystem simultaneously.
questions.explanation: The symptoms described — drifting toward generic "standard patterns" and contradicting earlier specific findings after a long, read-heavy session — are classic context degradation, where early details compete with the model's general knowledge and are lost to positional-attention effects as the window fills; the fix is having the agent persist key findings to an external scratchpad file that can be re-referenced and re-injected, so details survive context pressure. The rate-limit explanation is wrong because rate limits produce throttling errors, not gradual content drift, and adding delays does nothing for accuracy. Splitting the work across parallel subagents adds concurrency but does not solve within-session degradation, and swapping Read for Grep is a minor tooling tweak that neither halts context growth nor restores lost findings.

questions.task_desc: Context management in large codebase exploration
questions.stem: You are running a multi-phase codebase investigation using the Claude Agent SDK. After 45 minutes of exploration (reading files, tracing call chains, building a dependency map) you move into the second phase: identifying security vulnerabilities in the authentication module. You notice the agent is now describing the authentication module as "using standard JWT patterns" when earlier in the session it had found a custom token signing implementation. What technique would most effectively preserve key findings across these phase boundaries?
options: Have the agent maintain a scratchpad file that records key findings after each phase, and reference that file at the start of subsequent phases rather than relying on conversation history.
Increase the `max_tokens` parameter so the model can hold more context without compressing earlier findings.
Use `/compact` before starting the second phase to free up context space and allow the agent to re-read files as needed.
Restart the session with a fresh context at the start of each phase, passing a manual summary of what you found.
questions.explanation: The scenario shows context degradation: a specific earlier finding (the custom token signing implementation) is being lost as the context window fills. Writing key findings to an external scratchpad file and re-reading it at each phase boundary is the recommended agent-memory technique because it persists structured findings outside the fragile conversation history that gets compressed. Compacting conversation history frees space but summarizes away exactly the kind of specific detail that was lost, so it does not reliably preserve it; restarting with a fresh context each phase can work but discards accumulated understanding and needs manual re-summarizing; and raising the output-token limit only governs response length, not the context window, so it does nothing to prevent the degradation.

questions.task_desc: Human review workflows and confidence calibration
questions.stem: A structured data extraction system for insurance claims achieves 97% overall accuracy on a validation set. The team proposes automating the full workflow without human review. A colleague argues this aggregate metric masks important risks. What is the most important concern?
options: Aggregate accuracy metrics can mask poor performance on specific document types or fields. A claim type or field category with 85% accuracy would be hidden by strong performance elsewhere, and stratified analysis is needed before automating.
Human review should always be retained regardless of accuracy metrics because automation of insurance decisions creates regulatory risk.
97% accuracy means 3% of claims will have errors, which could create legal liability if unchecked.
The validation set may not be representative of production data volumes, so the 97% figure cannot be trusted.
questions.explanation: The colleague's specific concern is that a single aggregate figure can hide low accuracy on particular segments, so the strongest answer is the one calling for stratified analysis by document type and field before automating (a critical field at 85% accuracy stays invisible when other segments perform strongly). The choice that simply restates the 3% error rate as legal liability describes a consequence but not the masking mechanism being raised. The representativeness/data-volume choice conflates production volume with distribution and addresses a different risk. The blanket "always keep humans regardless of metrics" choice is a policy stance that never engages with the analytical gap in the aggregate metric.

questions.task_desc: Human review workflows and confidence calibration
questions.stem: An extraction pipeline achieves high accuracy on average but the team wants to implement confidence-based routing: high-confidence extractions proceed automatically while low-confidence ones go to human review. A developer proposes using the model's stated confidence scores directly as the routing threshold. What is the critical flaw in this approach?
options: Model confidence scores must be calibrated against a labeled validation set to determine what score threshold actually corresponds to acceptable accuracy. Uncalibrated raw scores are not reliable predictors of extraction correctness.
Routing on confidence scores creates two separate code paths that are harder to maintain than a single uniform review workflow.
Confidence scores only work when the model outputs a single extraction; multi-field documents require a different approach.
Confidence scores add tokens to every response, increasing API costs unnecessarily.
questions.explanation: A model's self-reported confidence scores are not inherently calibrated, so there is no reliable mapping between a stated score like "0.85" and an actual error rate until you compare stated confidence against ground truth on a labeled validation set; only then can a routing threshold be tied to a known accuracy level. The choice framing this calibration requirement is therefore the critical flaw. The claim that confidence scores merely add token cost is a minor operational concern rather than a correctness problem; the claim that scores only work for single-field extractions is false since confidence can be evaluated per field; and the concern about two code paths being harder to maintain is an engineering trade-off, not a flaw in confidence-based routing itself.

questions.stem: A research synthesis agent is combining findings from five subagents that searched different sources. The final report states that "AI adoption in healthcare reached 45% in 2024" but does not cite which source this came from. A reviewer cannot verify the claim because the subagents' individual outputs were not preserved. What structural change to the pipeline would prevent this provenance loss?
options: Require subagents to output structured claim-source mappings (claim text, source URL, document name, relevant excerpt) and instruct the synthesis agent to preserve and merge these mappings into the final report rather than summarizing them away.
Add a post-synthesis review step where a separate agent checks each claim in the report against the raw subagent outputs.
Store all raw subagent outputs in a separate log file so they can be consulted if a claim needs verification.
Require the synthesis agent to add a generic "Sources consulted" section at the end of the report listing all sources accessed.
questions.explanation: Provenance loss happens because summarization compresses findings and drops the link between each claim and its source; the fix is structural, requiring subagents to emit per-claim structured mappings (claim text, source URL, document, excerpt) that the synthesis agent must preserve and merge so every claim in the report carries its attribution. A generic "sources consulted" list names sources in aggregate but never ties an individual claim to a specific one, and stashing raw outputs in a separate log only provides a fallback rather than embedding provenance in the report. A post-hoc verification agent that re-checks claims against raw outputs catches errors after the fact but does not prevent the attribution from being lost in the first place.

questions.task_desc: Agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.stem:
You are implementing the agentic loop for a structured-extraction agent built on the Claude Agent SDK. After sending each request, your control-flow code must decide whether to run another iteration (executing the tool the model requested) or to return the final answer to the caller. You want the loop to be driven by the API's own signal of what the model intends to do next.

Which field should your control flow inspect, and how should it behave?
options: Inspect `stop_reason`: continue the loop (execute the tool and append its result) while it equals `"tool_use"`, and stop when it equals `"end_turn"`.
Inspect `stop_reason`: stop the loop as soon as it equals `"tool_use"`, since a tool call means the model has produced its final structured output.
Inspect the assistant's text content for a natural-language phrase such as "done" or "final answer", and stop the loop when that phrase appears.
Inspect a running counter of iterations and stop once it reaches a fixed cap, treating the cap as the primary signal that the model has finished.
questions.explanation: The loop's termination should be driven by the API's structured `stop_reason` field: a value of `"tool_use"` means the model wants to call a tool, so the loop executes it, appends the result, and continues; a value of `"end_turn"` means the model is finished. Stopping on `"tool_use"` is backwards, since that value signals more work remains, not completion. Reading assistant text for a phrase and using an iteration counter as the primary stop are both anti-patterns the guide warns against.

questions.task_desc: Agentic loops for autonomous task execution
questions.stem:
A teammate is reviewing your Claude Code CI/CD triage agent, whose loop already terminates correctly by inspecting `stop_reason` for `"end_turn"`. They propose ALSO adding a hard limit: if the loop reaches 25 tool calls without the model producing `end_turn`, break out and return whatever partial result exists, logging a warning. They ask whether this addition is acceptable.

How should you characterize adding this limit?
options: It is acceptable only if the cap replaces the `stop_reason` check entirely, since a fixed number of tool calls is a more predictable stopping rule.
It is unacceptable because the loop should instead scan the assistant's text for a completion phrase as a secondary confirmation before stopping.
It is unacceptable under any circumstances, because a correct agentic loop must rely solely on `stop_reason` and adding any iteration limit is always an anti-pattern.
It is acceptable as a safety guardrail against runaway loops, as long as `stop_reason` remains the primary termination mechanism and the cap is only a backstop.
questions.explanation: An iteration cap is legitimately used as a safety guardrail to prevent runaway loops, provided the model's `stop_reason` remains the primary way the loop decides to terminate. The problem the guide flags is using the cap AS the primary stop mechanism, not having one at all, so calling any cap forbidden is too strong. Replacing the `stop_reason` check with a fixed count would make the cap primary (the actual anti-pattern), and scanning assistant text for a phrase is itself a separate anti-pattern.

questions.stem: You are building a customer-support system with a coordinator agent and a billing subagent. The coordinator has already collected the customer's verified account ID, their stated refund amount, and the transaction date over several turns. When it delegates "process this refund" to the billing subagent, the subagent responds asking for the account ID and transaction details that the customer already provided. The coordinator's conversation clearly contains all of this. Why is the subagent asking again?
options: The billing subagent needs its own direct connection to the conversation session before it can read prior turns
The coordinator must lower the subagent's temperature so it stops re-requesting information it already has access to
Subagents inherit the full coordinator history by default, so the account ID was likely overwritten by a later turn and must be re-collected from the customer
Subagents run with isolated context and do not automatically inherit the coordinator's conversation history, so the required details must be passed explicitly in the delegation prompt
questions.explanation: In the coordinator-subagent (hub-and-spoke) pattern, subagents have isolated context and do NOT automatically inherit the coordinator's conversation history. Any facts the subagent needs (account ID, amount, date) must be explicitly included in the delegation prompt. The idea that subagents inherit full history by default is exactly the misconception being tested, and temperature or session-connection changes do not address context that was simply never passed in.

questions.stem: In a multi-agent research system, the web-search subagent discovers a key source that the document-analysis subagent should examine next, and the synthesis subagent then needs both of their outputs. A junior engineer proposes wiring the subagents to message each other directly to pass these results along, forming a chain. Per the coordinator-subagent pattern, what is the problem with this design?
options: Subagents should each independently decide which other subagents to invoke, so the coordinator only needs to handle the final report
Direct subagent-to-subagent messaging is fine as long as each subagent also copies the coordinator on every message it sends
In a hub-and-spoke pattern the coordinator manages all inter-subagent communication and routing; subagents should not talk directly to each other
Chaining subagents directly is the recommended approach because it removes the coordinator as a bottleneck for passing intermediate results
questions.explanation: The defining property of the hub-and-spoke coordinator-subagent pattern is that the coordinator manages all inter-subagent communication, routing, and error handling. Subagents communicate through the coordinator, not directly with one another. Letting subagents message each other (even with the coordinator copied) or letting each subagent decide who to invoke abandons the hub-and-spoke structure that centralizes routing and aggregation.

questions.stem: A coordinator agent dispatches three subagents to analyze market trends, competitor pricing, and customer sentiment for a quarterly report. Each subagent returns its own findings independently. Which responsibility belongs to the coordinator rather than to any individual subagent in this pattern?
options: Aggregating the subagents' separate findings into a unified result and dynamically deciding which subagents to invoke
Ensuring every query is always routed to all three subagents to guarantee complete coverage
Performing its analysis strictly in a fixed order the subagents negotiate among themselves
Inheriting each subagent's private context automatically so no explicit handoff is ever needed
questions.explanation: In the coordinator-subagent pattern the coordinator owns decomposition, delegation, aggregation, and dynamically selects which subagents to invoke for a given request. Automatic context inheritance is explicitly not how the pattern works. Always routing to every subagent is the over-decomposition/over-routing anti-pattern, and routing decisions are made by the coordinator, not negotiated among subagents.

questions.stem: A coordinator agent has been debugging a payments service for several turns and has built up rich context: the failing endpoint, the stack trace, and the customer's account tier. It now spawns a subagent via the `Task` tool with the prompt "Investigate the root cause and propose a fix." The subagent immediately asks which endpoint is failing and reports it has no stack trace to work from. The coordinator's `allowedTools` correctly includes `"Task"`. Why is the subagent unable to proceed?
options: The subagent inherits history only if it is spawned in the same response turn as the coordinator's most recent tool result; spawning it a turn later drops the context
The coordinator must first call `fork_session` so the subagent branches from the shared debugging baseline before the Task call will carry context
The subagent's `AgentDefinition` is missing a `parentContext` field that links it to the coordinator's session so history can be replayed
Subagents run in isolated context and do not automatically inherit the coordinator's conversation history, so the endpoint, stack trace, and account tier must be passed explicitly in the Task prompt
questions.explanation: Subagents operate with isolated context and do NOT automatically inherit the coordinator's conversation history, so any facts they need (endpoint, stack trace, account tier) must be stated explicitly in the Task prompt. There is no automatic history-replay field on the agent definition, and forking sessions is a separate mechanism for branching an analysis baseline within a session, not for handing context to a freshly spawned subagent. Whether the spawn happens in the same turn affects parallelism, not context inheritance.

questions.stem: You are designing a multi-agent research system. You want each subagent to have its own focused system prompt, a curated description so the coordinator knows when to invoke it, and a restricted set of tools (for example, the citation subagent should not have write access). Where do these per-subagent descriptions, system prompts, and tool restrictions belong?
options: They are injected automatically by the coordinator at spawn time based on the subtopic, so no explicit definition is required
In each subagent's `AgentDefinition`, which holds the description, system prompt, and tool restrictions for that subagent
They must be placed in the coordinator's own system prompt as a numbered list, since subagents cannot hold independent configuration
In a project-level `.mcp.json` file, which is where tool restrictions and agent descriptions are declared for all agents
questions.explanation: The `AgentDefinition` is the structure that holds a subagent's description, system prompt, and tool restrictions. These are not auto-generated at spawn time, nor are they crammed into the coordinator's own prompt. A `.mcp.json` file configures MCP servers and their tools, not per-subagent descriptions and system prompts.

questions.task_desc: Multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff
questions.stem: A support agent resolves most tickets autonomously but must escalate to a human specialist when it hits a policy gap. Today the agent escalates by writing a one-line note like "Customer is upset, please help." The human specialists complain they have to re-read the entire transcript and re-diagnose every case from scratch. Which change to the handoff would most improve specialist efficiency without sacrificing accuracy?
options: Forward only the customer's most recent message so the specialist can form an unbiased opinion
Have the agent produce a structured handoff containing the customer's verified details, the root cause it identified, and its recommended next actions
Attach a sentiment score and the agent's self-reported confidence level so specialists can triage which escalations are most urgent
Instruct the agent via system prompt to "always write a thorough, detailed escalation summary"
questions.explanation: A structured escalation handoff should carry the concrete artifacts a human needs to act: verified customer details, the identified root cause, and recommended actions. That is exactly what lets a specialist pick up the case without re-diagnosing it. Forwarding only the latest message discards the diagnosis already done, and sentiment or self-reported confidence scores are unreliable proxies rather than the substantive context a handoff needs. A vague prompt instruction to be thorough does not specify the fields that make a handoff useful and retains a non-zero failure rate.

questions.task_desc: Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception
questions.stem: You are designing hooks for a customer support agent built on the Agent SDK. You have two requirements: (1) before any `issue_refund` tool call actually runs, verify the refund amount is under the policy ceiling and block it otherwise; and (2) after the `get_account` tool returns, convert its Unix-timestamp fields to ISO 8601 so the model reasons about dates correctly. Which combination correctly maps each requirement to the right hook interception point?
options: Requirement 1 intercepts the tool result after the refund executes and reverses it if non-compliant; requirement 2 intercepts the outgoing call to reformat the request
Both requirements must intercept the tool result after execution, since hooks can only observe outputs, not inputs
Both requirements should be handled by intercepting the outgoing tool call, because that is the only point where a hook can modify data
Requirement 1 intercepts the OUTGOING tool call before execution to enforce compliance; requirement 2 intercepts the tool RESULT after execution to normalize it before the model sees it
questions.explanation: Compliance enforcement (blocking a non-compliant refund) must happen on the OUTGOING tool call before execution, so the disallowed action never runs. Format normalization operates on the tool RESULT after execution, transforming it before the model sees it. Reversing a refund after it already executed is unsafe and not how the enforcement pattern works, and normalization cannot happen on outputs if it is applied to the request instead. Hooks intercept both directions, so the claim that they only observe outputs is false.

questions.task_desc: Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception
questions.stem: A support agent must enforce this policy: any refund request above $500 must be routed to a human escalation queue rather than processed. Prompt instructions alone currently let a few of these through. Your team wants the interception hook to do more than just deny the call. What is the recommended behavior for the hook that intercepts the outgoing `process_refund` call?
options: Silently drop the tool call and let the agent continue, so the customer is not told anything happened
Return a random subset of over-limit refunds to a human while auto-approving the rest to reduce escalation volume
Allow the refund to process but log a warning for later manual review by a supervisor
Block the over-limit call and redirect it to the escalation path so the request is handed off to a human
questions.explanation: Hooks that intercept outgoing tool calls enforce compliance by blocking the disallowed action AND redirecting it to escalation, so an over-limit refund becomes a human handoff rather than an autonomous action. Silently dropping the call leaves the customer's issue unresolved and hides the event, allowing the refund to process defeats the guarantee, and randomly sampling which over-limit refunds to escalate violates the policy for the ones it lets through.

questions.stem:
You are designing a Claude review over a pull request that touches eight files. You want a predictable structure that first analyzes each file on its own, then reasons about how the files interact. A colleague argues that because 'reviewing code always requires judgment,' the whole thing should be left to fully open-ended, dynamically-decided decomposition instead of a planned pipeline.

How should prompt chaining be applied here, and what is the flaw in the colleague's reasoning?
options: Use dynamic decomposition for the per-file steps and a fixed pipeline only for the final summary
Chain the review as sequential steps — analyze each file, then run a cross-file integration pass; the colleague conflates needing judgment within steps with needing an unpredictable overall structure
Skip chaining entirely and let the model dynamically decide the review structure each run, since the colleague is right that all code review is open-ended
Run one prompt per file only, with no cross-file step, because integrating findings across files is out of scope for prompt chaining
questions.explanation: Prompt chaining breaks a predictable multi-aspect review into sequential steps: analyze each file, then run a cross-file integration pass. The multi-aspect structure is known in advance, so the overall pipeline is predictable even though each step still requires the model to reason. The colleague conflates 'the steps require judgment' with 'the overall structure is unknowable.' Dropping the cross-file pass would miss interactions between files, and there is no reason to make the predictable per-file steps dynamically decided.

questions.stem: A platform team runs a nightly Claude Code job that continues a long-lived investigation into a flaky integration test suite. Each night's run needs to pick up exactly where the previous run left off, so an engineer named the session 'flaky-suite-triage' when it was first created. In the CI script, what is the correct way to continue that same named session on subsequent runs?
options: Use `fork_session flaky-suite-triage` so the run inherits the prior investigation state
Invoke Claude Code with `--resume flaky-suite-triage` to reattach to the named session
Start a fresh session each night and rely on Claude Code to auto-detect the prior 'flaky-suite-triage' session by name
Re-run with no session flags; Claude Code resumes the most recent session with a matching topic automatically
questions.explanation: Named session resumption is done explicitly with the `--resume <session-name>` flag, so passing the session's name reattaches to the exact prior session and its accumulated state. There is no automatic detection or topic-matching that reconnects a fresh invocation to a prior session. Forking is for creating an independent divergent branch from a baseline, not for continuing the same line of work.

questions.stem: A developer resumed a two-day-old Claude Code session to keep working on a data-pipeline migration. During those two days, a teammate refactored the pipeline: several files the earlier session had read and cached in its tool results have been substantially rewritten, and the schema changed. When the developer resumes and asks Claude to continue, the answers reference the old structure. What is the best way to get productive, accurate work going again?
options: Resume the session and repeatedly ask Claude to double-check its assumptions until the old tool results are overwritten
Fork the stale session into two branches and compare which one produces more accurate answers
Start fresh with a structured summary of the current state, since the prior session's tool results are now stale
Trust the resumed session as-is; Claude will re-read the changed files automatically before answering
questions.explanation: When prior tool results are stale, starting fresh with a structured summary of the current state can outperform resuming, because the resumed session carries outdated cached results that bias the model's answers. A resumed agent does not automatically detect or re-read files that changed outside the session. Forking only duplicates the stale baseline, and prompting Claude to second-guess itself does not reliably purge the outdated cached results.

questions.task_desc: Tool interface design with clear descriptions
questions.stem: A dev-productivity agent has two well-named tools: `search_code` (searches the current repository's source files) and `search_docs` (searches the company's internal API documentation portal). Each description accurately says what its own tool does, but the agent keeps calling `search_docs` when a developer asks 'where is the retry logic implemented?' — a question that should hit the codebase. The descriptions never mention when to prefer one tool over the other. What is the most effective way to reduce this cross-tool confusion?
options: Add explicit 'use this vs that' boundary guidance to each description, e.g. clarify that `search_code` is for locating implementations in this repo while `search_docs` is for conceptual/API-reference questions
Add an instruction in the system prompt telling the agent to 'always try the docs first' before searching code
Merge `search_code` and `search_docs` into a single `search` tool that queries both sources and returns combined results
Set `tool_choice` to forced on `search_code` so the agent is required to call it first for every developer question
questions.explanation: When each tool's description is individually accurate but the agent still confuses overlapping-purpose tools, the missing piece is disambiguating boundary guidance — spelling out when to prefer one tool over the other. Merging the tools discards the useful separation and returns noisy combined results. Forcing one tool for every question breaks the legitimate cases where docs is the right target. A blanket 'docs first' system-prompt instruction pushes the agent the wrong direction and doesn't teach the actual boundary.

questions.task_desc: Tool interface design with clear descriptions
questions.stem: An engineer notices that ever since the phrase 'always keep the customer's billing status up to date' was added to a support agent's system prompt, the agent has started calling the `update_billing` tool during read-only account inquiries where no billing change was requested. The `update_billing` tool description itself is clear and scoped. What is the most likely cause of the spurious tool calls?
options: System-prompt keyword sensitivity created an unintended association between the prompt wording and the billing tool, biasing the agent toward invoking it
The `update_billing` description is too detailed, so the model over-weights it during selection
The tool needs a forced `tool_choice` of `"any"` to stop it from firing on read-only requests
MCP tool discovery loaded `update_billing` twice, so it appears more attractive to the selector
questions.explanation: Keywords in the system prompt can create unintended tool associations — here 'billing' phrasing in the prompt biases the model toward the billing-mutation tool even when no change is needed. The tool's own description being detailed is generally desirable and isn't the culprit. Setting tool_choice to 'any' would force a tool call on every turn, making the over-invocation worse, not better. Duplicate discovery isn't indicated and isn't how the association forms.

questions.task_desc: Tool interface design with clear descriptions
questions.stem: You are authoring the description for a `query_transactions` tool used by a financial-support agent. You want to maximize the chance the agent both selects it correctly and passes valid inputs on the first try. Beyond a one-line summary of what the tool does, which additional content in the description most directly improves reliable tool use per interface-design guidance?
options: Input formats, example queries the tool is meant to answer, and edge cases it does or doesn't cover
A note that the tool is 'very powerful and should be used carefully' to encourage conservative use
A changelog of every version of the tool and the names of the engineers who maintain it
The full backend SQL schema and the internal microservice endpoint the tool calls
questions.explanation: Effective tool descriptions include concrete input formats, representative example queries, and edge-case coverage so the agent selects the tool at the right moments and supplies well-formed inputs. Vague cautionary language like 'use carefully' gives the model nothing actionable. Internal schema and endpoint details are implementation trivia the model doesn't need for selection. Maintainer names and changelogs are irrelevant to routing or input formatting.

questions.task_desc: Structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.stem: In your multi-agent research system, a data-retrieval subagent calls an MCP tool that hits a momentary connection timeout on its first attempt but succeeds on an immediate second attempt. Currently the subagent forwards every raw error straight to the coordinator, which halts the whole run whenever any subagent reports a problem. You want to reduce noise while keeping the coordinator able to make good decisions. How should transient failures like this timeout be handled?
options: Have the coordinator terminate the entire workflow on the first error from any subagent to guarantee consistency
Have the subagent attempt local recovery for transient failures and only propagate errors it genuinely cannot resolve, including any partial results it did collect
Forward all errors verbatim to the coordinator so the coordinator alone owns every recovery decision
Have the subagent silently swallow all failures and return an empty result set so the run never halts
questions.explanation: Transient failures such as timeouts are best resolved locally inside the subagent, which then propagates only what it cannot resolve, along with any partial results, so the coordinator makes informed choices. Forwarding every raw error pushes recoverable noise upward and defeats the purpose. Silently swallowing failures and returning empty results hides real outages and is an anti-pattern. Terminating the whole workflow on any single failure is exactly the brittleness this design avoids.

questions.task_desc: Structured error responses for MCP tools
questions.stem: You are designing structured error responses for an order-management MCP tool. Two distinct failures need different agent behavior: (1) the request included a malformed date string that the tool could not parse, and (2) the requesting agent's credentials do not grant access to the customer's account. If both returned an identical generic error, what categorization would best let the agent respond correctly to each?
options: Label the malformed date as a validation error and the credential problem as a permission error, each with its own errorCategory and description
Label both as business/policy errors so the agent escalates every failure to a human reviewer
Mark both as transient with isRetryable true, since retrying is the safest default for any failure
Return only a human-readable message and let the agent infer the category from the wording
questions.explanation: The guide distinguishes error categories including validation (bad input, like the unparseable date) and permission (access not granted), and each should carry a structured errorCategory plus a human-readable description so the agent can fix input versus stop retrying and seek authorization. Marking both transient with isRetryable true would cause pointless retries of a bad input and an unauthorized call. Treating both as business/policy errors mis-routes them and floods escalation. Relying on the agent to infer category from free-text wording is the weakness structured metadata is meant to remove.

questions.task_desc: Tool distribution across agents and tool_choice
questions.stem: A triage agent in your customer support system should always take an action on each turn — it must call one of route_to_billing, route_to_technical, or ask_clarifying_question — but you deliberately want the model to decide WHICH of the three fits the incoming ticket. During testing with the default configuration, the agent sometimes just replies with conversational text like 'Sure, I can help with that' and never calls any tool. Which tool_choice setting fixes this while still letting the model pick among the three tools?
options: Force tool_choice to a specific tool such as {"type":"tool","name":"route_to_billing"} and let the agent re-route later
Set tool_choice to "any" so the model must call one of the available tools but chooses which
Set tool_choice to "auto" so the model reliably selects the best-fitting tool
Leave tool_choice unset and add an instruction in the system prompt telling the model it must always call a tool
questions.explanation: The requirement is 'call some tool every turn, but let the model choose which one.' The setting that guarantees a tool call while leaving the specific choice to the model is the one that forces any tool. The auto setting is exactly what allowed the plain-text replies, so it does not fix the problem. Forcing one specific named tool removes the model's ability to pick among the three. A prompt instruction only makes compliance likely, not guaranteed — the whole point of the setting is a hard guarantee.

questions.task_desc: Tool distribution across agents and tool_choice
questions.stem: An extraction agent has three tools: extract_fields, request_document, and reply_to_user. For most invocations you actually want the model to have the freedom to either call a tool OR just respond in natural language when the user is only chatting (e.g., saying 'thanks'). A teammate proposes hard-forcing tool_choice to extract_fields on every request 'to be safe.' Why is the default auto setting the better fit here, and what does it allow?
