options: auto behaves identically to "any", so either choice is fine for this agent
auto forces the specific extract_fields tool but still permits text afterward
auto guarantees a tool is always called, which is what you want for every turn
auto lets the model either call a tool or return plain text, matching the need to sometimes just reply conversationally
questions.explanation: With the auto setting the model may call a tool or return a plain-text response, which is exactly right when some turns are just conversational and should not trigger extraction. Forcing extract_fields on every request would misfire on turns like a simple thank-you. The auto setting does not guarantee a tool call — that is the behavior of the setting that requires any tool. And auto is not the same as the must-call-a-tool setting; the difference is precisely whether plain text is allowed.

questions.task_desc: MCP server integration into Claude Code
questions.stem:
You are experimenting with a personal MCP server that wraps a scratch note-taking API you use for your own workflow. It is not relevant to the rest of the team and you don't want it to show up when a teammate clones the repository. A separate MCP server that wraps the team's internal ticketing system SHOULD be available to everyone who clones the repo.

How should you configure these two MCP servers?
options: Put the personal note-taking server in your user-level ~/.claude.json and the team ticketing server in the project-level .mcp.json committed to the repo
Put both servers in your user-level ~/.claude.json so nothing personal is ever committed, and share setup instructions in the README
Put the personal note-taking server in .mcp.json but add it to .gitignore so it stays local
Put both servers in the project-level .mcp.json so they are version-controlled together, and have each engineer comment out the ones they don't want
questions.explanation: Personal or experimental servers belong in the user-level configuration so they stay local to you and are never distributed to teammates, while shared team tooling belongs in the project-level .mcp.json that is committed so everyone who clones the repo gets it automatically. Committing both to the project file would force the personal server on teammates. Putting both in the user-level file would fail to share the team ticketing server. Gitignoring the project file defeats the purpose of the project-level config, which exists precisely to be shared.

questions.task_desc: MCP server integration into Claude Code
questions.stem:
An engineer has three MCP servers configured for a project: one for GitHub, one for a documentation store, and one for a metrics database. They ask whether they need to tell Claude which server to activate at the start of a session, or switch between them as the task changes.

Which statement correctly describes how the tools from these servers become available?
options: Tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and are all available simultaneously without manual switching
Only the first server listed in the configuration connects at startup; the others must be activated explicitly with a switch command when needed
Claude loads one server per task based on the user's phrasing and unloads it before connecting to the next server
Each server must be selected via tool_choice before its tools can be discovered by the model
questions.explanation: When Claude Code connects to its configured MCP servers, the tools from all of them are discovered at connection time and are available at the same time, so no manual activation or switching between servers is needed. There is no first-only loading, no one-server-per-task swapping, and tool_choice governs whether/which tool is called within a request, not whether a server's tools are discovered at all.

questions.task_desc: MCP server integration into Claude Code
questions.stem:
Your team wants Claude Code to be able to read and update issues in Jira, which is a widely used standard integration. An engineer proposes writing a custom MCP server from scratch that reimplements the Jira REST calls your team needs.

What does the guidance recommend for a standard integration like this?
options: Expose the Jira endpoints as MCP resources instead of tools so no server code is required
Prefer an existing community MCP server for the standard integration rather than building a bespoke one
Skip MCP entirely and paste Jira data into the prompt manually to avoid maintaining any server
Build the custom server from scratch so the tool surface exactly matches only the endpoints your team uses
questions.explanation: For common, standard integrations such as Jira, the recommendation is to prefer an existing community MCP server rather than reinventing one, since a bespoke build adds maintenance burden without added value for a well-supported integration. Building from scratch is the anti-pattern being warned against here. Pasting data manually forgoes the benefits of tool integration, and resources expose content catalogs to reduce exploratory calls but are not a substitute for the action-taking tools an integration like this requires, nor do they eliminate the need for a server.

questions.task_desc: Built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob)
questions.stem: A dev-productivity agent is asked to understand how request authentication flows through a large, unfamiliar backend repository before proposing a change. The engineer tells the agent to "figure out how auth works here." Which approach best matches the recommended way to build codebase understanding with the built-in tools?
options: Use Edit to add temporary logging statements throughout the codebase and run it to observe the auth flow at runtime
Use Grep to locate the auth entry points (e.g., the middleware or handler names), then Read those files and follow their imports to trace the flow incrementally
Read every file under the repository root sequentially so the agent has complete context before answering
Use Glob to list all file paths in the repo and infer the auth design from the directory and file names alone
questions.explanation: The guide says to build codebase understanding incrementally: Grep for entry points, then Read to follow imports, rather than reading everything. Exhaustively reading every file wastes context and is the anti-pattern the guide warns against. Listing paths with Glob shows structure but not behavior, and Edit is for making targeted changes, not for exploration.

questions.task_desc: Built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob)
questions.stem: An agent needs to collect every Jest test file that follows the `*.test.tsx` naming convention across a React monorepo so it can run them as a group. It does not yet care about the contents of the files, only which paths exist. Which built-in tool is designed for this job?
options: Grep, searching the file contents for the string `describe(` to identify test files
Glob, matching a file-path pattern such as `**/*.test.tsx`
Read, opening each candidate directory to enumerate its test files
Edit, because locating a set of matching files is a targeted change operation
questions.explanation: Glob is the file-path pattern-matching tool, and **/*.test.tsx is exactly the kind of pattern it is meant for. Grep searches file contents for patterns rather than matching path names, so it is the wrong fit when you only need paths. Read operates on individual files, and Edit performs targeted in-file changes, neither of which enumerates a matching set of paths.

questions.stem: Your team's root `CLAUDE.md` bundles four large sections inline: a security-review checklist, database migration conventions, API-design guidelines, and release procedures. You want to keep each section in its own file so they can be reviewed and versioned independently, while still having Claude Code load all of them as part of the project's memory in every session. What is the cleanest way to achieve this?
options: Move each section into `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` so the content is centralized and automatically shared with every teammate who clones the repo
Split the sections into four separate `.claude/rules/` files with `paths:` globs so only the relevant one activates per session
Move each section into its own file and reference them from the root `CLAUDE.md` using `@import` syntax, keeping the main file modular while still loading everything
Keep the four sections inline in the root `CLAUDE.md` but wrap each in HTML comment markers, since separate files are only loaded when you are editing matching paths
questions.explanation: The guide states that `@import` syntax references external files to keep CLAUDE.md modular, which lets each section live in its own versioned file while still being loaded as project memory. Wrapping sections in comment markers does not split them into separate files and is not how loading works. The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is explicitly NOT shared via VCS, so it would not reach teammates. Path-scoped `.claude/rules/` files load only when editing matching files, so they would not all load in every session as required here.

questions.stem: A developer just added a directory-level `CLAUDE.md` inside `services/payments/` and also updated the project root `CLAUDE.md`. Before starting a complex refactor in the payments service, they want to confirm exactly which memory files Claude Code has actually loaded for the current session so they can be sure both the project-level and directory-level guidance are in effect. What should they use?
options: Restart Claude Code with the `--verbose` flag, since memory files are only loaded on a clean restart
Check `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, because directory-level files are merged into the user-level file at load time
Ask Claude in the chat to summarize its system prompt, since loaded memory is the only reliable signal
Run the `/memory` command, which reports which memory files are currently loaded
questions.explanation: The guide explicitly lists the `/memory` command as the way to verify which memory files are loaded. There is no described `--verbose`-on-restart requirement for memory loading. Asking the model to summarize its system prompt is not the described verification mechanism and is unreliable. Directory-level files are not merged into the user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`, which is a separate, non-VCS-shared scope.

questions.stem: You are designing memory configuration for a large monorepo. The team wants general conventions to apply everywhere, but the `infra/` subtree has stricter, Terraform-specific rules that should take effect whenever someone works inside it, layered on top of the general project guidance. Which combination of memory scopes correctly expresses this hierarchy?
options: Put the general conventions in the project-level root `CLAUDE.md` and the Terraform-specific rules in a directory-level `infra/CLAUDE.md`
Put the Terraform-specific rules in the root `CLAUDE.md` and the general conventions in an `infra/CLAUDE.md`, since deeper files hold the broader defaults
Put both the general and Terraform-specific rules in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` so all developers get them automatically
Put all rules in a single user-level file and rely on sentiment cues in the prompt to decide which apply
questions.explanation: The guide describes a hierarchy of user-level, project-level (root/`.claude/CLAUDE.md`), and directory-level (subdirectory CLAUDE.md) memory; general project conventions belong at the project root and the narrower Terraform-specific rules belong in the directory-level `infra/CLAUDE.md`. Putting everything in `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` is the user-level scope, which is not shared via VCS and is not the way to express project-wide plus directory-scoped layering. Inverting the two files places broad defaults in the deep directory, which reverses the intended scoping. Relying on prompt sentiment cues is not a memory-scoping mechanism at all.

questions.stem: You are authoring a `/db-migrate` skill that walks Claude through generating and applying database migrations. Because a mistake here could be destructive, you want to guarantee that when this skill runs, Claude can only read files and run a narrow set of shell commands, and can never invoke the Write or Edit tools. Which SKILL.md frontmatter field enforces this restriction?
options: `context: fork`, which isolates the skill's execution in a separate sub-agent context
`allowed-tools`, which restricts which tools the skill is permitted to access
`paths`, which limits the skill to operating only on files matching a glob pattern
`argument-hint`, which prompts the user for the tools to permit at invocation time
questions.explanation: The frontmatter field that restricts which tools a skill can access is the one designed for exactly that purpose, letting you exclude Write and Edit and permit only Read plus a narrow command set. Isolating context in a forked sub-agent prevents output from polluting the main conversation but does nothing to constrain the tool set. Prompting for parameters at invocation and glob-based conditional activation of rule files are unrelated mechanisms and do not gate tool access.

questions.stem: A teammate has committed a `/deploy` skill to your repository's `.claude/skills/` directory. You want your own personalized version that adds extra staging-environment safety checks and a different confirmation flow, but you do NOT want to overwrite or conflict with the shared team skill everyone relies on. What is the recommended approach?
options: Create your personal variant in `~/.claude/skills/` using a different name so it does not collide with the shared skill
Add a second SKILL.md file with the same name in `.claude/skills/` so both versions load side by side
Move the shared skill to `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` and reference it with `@import` from your personal config
Edit the shared `.claude/skills/deploy/SKILL.md` directly and add your personal checks behind a conditional
questions.explanation: Personal skill variants belong in the user-level skills directory and should use a different name so they coexist with, rather than clobber, the project-scoped shared skill. Editing the committed team skill would change it for everyone and defeats the goal of a personal variant. Two identically named skills in the same project directory collide rather than loading side by side, and CLAUDE.md with @import is a memory mechanism, not a place to host skills.

questions.stem: You are building a `/jira-ticket` slash command that should let a developer type `/jira-ticket PROJ-1234` and have Claude fetch and summarize that ticket. During testing, developers who run the command without knowing the expected input just type `/jira-ticket` and are confused about what to supply. Which frontmatter setting is designed to surface the expected parameter to the user?
options: `argument-hint`, which prompts for the expected parameters
`allowed-tools`, which lists the parameters the command is allowed to accept
`context: fork`, which shows the user a preview of the isolated context before running
`paths`, which declares the glob patterns the command activates on
questions.explanation: The frontmatter field intended to prompt the user for expected parameters is the argument-hint field, which is exactly what tells developers what input like a ticket key to provide. Forking context isolates execution to avoid polluting the main conversation and does not preview or prompt for arguments. Restricting the tool set and glob-based path activation serve entirely different purposes and do not communicate expected parameters to the user.

questions.task_desc: Path-specific rules for conditional convention loading
questions.stem: A platform team maintains a monolithic root `CLAUDE.md` that has grown to hundreds of lines. Much of it is convention detail that only matters when Claude edits GraphQL schema files (`*.graphql`) or database migration files under `db/migrations/`. Engineers report that unrelated frontend sessions feel noisy and that Claude occasionally applies migration rules to plain TypeScript. What change best addresses both problems?
options: Move the conventions into a user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` so they are available across all projects the engineer works on
Split the conventions into subdirectory `CLAUDE.md` files placed in the GraphQL and migrations folders, relying on directory-level memory to scope them
Keep everything in the root `CLAUDE.md` but add a prose instruction telling Claude to ignore migration rules unless it is editing migration files

Move the GraphQL and migration conventions into separate files under `.claude/rules/` with a YAML frontmatter `paths:` field listing the relevant glob patterns, so each rule set loads only when matching files are edited
questions.explanation: Path-scoped rule files under `.claude/rules/` use a YAML frontmatter `paths:` field with glob patterns so the conventions activate only when Claude edits matching files, which removes the noise from unrelated sessions and stops rules from bleeding into unrelated file types. Subdirectory CLAUDE.md files can scope by directory but the guide prefers glob-pattern rules for conventions like these, and they still load whenever that directory is in play rather than keying on file type. A prose instruction to ignore rules is exactly the kind of always-loaded, non-deterministic approach path scoping is meant to replace. A user-level CLAUDE.md is not shared via VCS and would apply everywhere, worsening the noise.

questions.task_desc: Path-specific rules for conditional convention loading
questions.stem: You author a path-specific rule file `.claude/rules/react-tests.md` with frontmatter `paths: ["**/*.test.tsx"]` describing your React Testing Library conventions. During a session, Claude opens `src/utils/date.ts` to fix a helper function and never touches any `.test.tsx` file. A teammate worries the testing conventions will pollute this unrelated edit. What actually happens?
options: The rule causes an error because the edited file does not match the declared path pattern
The rule always loads because any file under `.claude/rules/` is treated as global project memory regardless of its `paths:` field
The rule stays inactive for this edit because no file matching the `**/*.test.tsx` glob is being worked on, so the testing conventions do not load into context
The rule loads because `date.ts` lives under `src/`, and the glob matches any file in the source tree
questions.explanation: Path-scoped rules load only when the file being edited matches one of the glob patterns in the `paths:` frontmatter; editing `date.ts` matches nothing in `**/*.test.tsx`, so the testing conventions simply stay inactive. Files in `.claude/rules/` are not unconditionally global when they carry a `paths:` field, that field is precisely what makes them conditional. The glob `**/*.test.tsx` matches only files ending in `.test.tsx`, not everything under `src/`. A non-matching edit is the normal case, not an error condition.

questions.stem: A developer is about to add a new authentication provider to a service. The overall shape of the work is uncertain — there are two or three plausible integration points and some existing middleware they haven't read yet — but each individual code change, once the approach is settled, is small and mechanical. They want to move efficiently without re-deriving the whole design on every edit. Which workflow best fits this task?
options: Skip both and immediately open a session cap of a few iterations to force the work to finish quickly
Use plan mode for the entire task, keeping every edit inside plan mode so nothing is committed until the very end
Use plan mode to investigate the codebase and settle on an integration approach, then switch to direct execution to carry out the well-scoped edits
Use direct execution from the start, since the individual edits are small and mechanical
questions.explanation: The task has genuine up-front uncertainty (unread middleware, multiple integration points) but mechanical edits once the approach is chosen, which is exactly the hybrid the guide describes: plan mode for investigation and design, then direct execution for the well-scoped implementation. Jumping straight to direct execution risks committing to a bad integration point before understanding the middleware. Staying in plan mode for the whole task ignores that the edits are simple and well-scoped once decided. An iteration cap is a safety guardrail, not a workflow-selection mechanism.

questions.stem: Two engineers debate how to handle a request to introduce a caching layer that could reasonably be implemented at the repository layer, the service layer, or via a decorator. One argues they should just start editing in direct execution and adjust as they go. Why does the guide favor plan mode here instead?
options: Because direct execution is only permitted for read-only tasks, and adding a cache writes files
Because plan mode lets the team explore and design the approach before committing, which is the right fit when there are multiple valid architectural approaches
Because plan mode guarantees the implementation will be bug-free, unlike direct execution
Because plan mode runs the changes on a separate branch automatically, whereas direct execution edits the live branch
questions.explanation: The defining trait here is multiple valid architectural approaches (repository, service, or decorator), and the guide reserves plan mode precisely for complex tasks with several valid approaches and architectural decisions, enabling safe exploration and design before committing. Direct execution is not limited to read-only work — it is for simple, well-scoped changes. Plan mode is not a branching or version-control mechanism, and it makes no correctness guarantee about the resulting code.

questions.stem: During a code review, a teammate claims that plan mode is unnecessary overhead for a small task like renaming a single local variable inside one function, and that the same reasoning means plan mode should be avoided for a sweeping rename that touches dozens of files and public interfaces across many modules. How should you respond?
options: Disagree fully — both renames should use plan mode, because any rename can have unexpected effects
Agree fully — plan mode is overhead in both cases, so direct execution is correct for both the single-variable rename and the cross-module rename
Both should use plan mode, but only if the files exceed a fixed line-count threshold
The teammate is right about the single-variable rename (direct execution), but the cross-module rename is a large-scale multi-file change where plan mode is appropriate
questions.explanation: The guide draws the line by task scope, not by the word 'rename': a single, well-scoped change like renaming one local variable fits direct execution, while a sweeping change across dozens of files and public interfaces is a large-scale, multi-file modification that plan mode is meant for. Treating both identically ignores that distinction in either direction. There is no fixed line-count threshold in the guide; the criteria are scope, number of files, and whether architectural or multi-approach decisions are involved.

questions.stem: A developer is using Claude Code to implement a parser for a proprietary log format. The exact edge-case behavior (how to handle malformed timestamps, empty fields, and unicode in message bodies) is hard to describe precisely in prose, and the developer has already written a clear specification twice with mixed results. The developer already has a suite of expected-behavior assertions for these edge cases. What is the most effective iterative refinement technique here?
options: Ask Claude to lower its confidence threshold and be more conservative when handling malformed input
Rewrite the prose specification a third time with even more explicit wording about each edge case
Switch to plan mode so Claude can architect the parser before writing any code
Adopt a test-driven iteration loop: have Claude write or run the tests first, then iterate by sharing the specific test failures until they pass
questions.explanation: When correct behavior is captured by concrete assertions, test-driven iteration is the recommended technique: establish the tests, then iterate by feeding Claude the specific failures so it converges on passing behavior. Rewriting prose a third time repeats an approach that already gave mixed results, since prose is being interpreted inconsistently. Plan mode addresses architectural exploration, not edge-case correctness with existing tests. 'Be more conservative'/lowering a confidence threshold is a vague instruction that does not reliably improve precision.

questions.stem: A team wires Claude Code into their CI pipeline so that immediately after an authoring run generates a feature's implementation, the SAME Claude Code session is asked to review the diff it just produced before opening the PR. Reviewers notice the CI review rarely flags logic gaps that a human catches minutes later. What change best improves the review's effectiveness?
options: Spawn a separate, independent Claude Code review instance with no prior context from the authoring run, rather than reusing the session that generated the code
Enable extended thinking on the existing session so it reasons more deeply before approving its own diff
Add a stricter system prompt telling the same session to 'be more critical and only approve high-confidence code'
Increase the iteration cap on the authoring session so it has more turns to reconsider its output
questions.explanation: A session that just generated the code retains its own reasoning context and is biased toward validating its earlier decisions, so it misses issues it already 'decided' were fine. Launching a fresh, independent review instance with no generation context catches more subtle problems. Extended thinking or more iterations on the same session does not remove that self-review bias, and vague 'be more critical' instructions do not reliably improve precision.

questions.stem: A nightly CI job runs Claude Code to scan the codebase for issues and files them. On the first run it reported 40 findings, most of which the team triaged and either fixed or accepted. On every subsequent nightly run Claude re-reports the same already-triaged findings, drowning out genuinely new issues. What is the best way to invoke Claude Code on the recurring runs?
options: Include the prior run's findings in the invocation so Claude reports only new or still-unaddressed issues
Lower the number of files scanned each night so fewer duplicate findings can surface
Randomize which subset of the codebase is scanned each night to avoid re-encountering the same code
Switch the recurring runs to interactive mode so a human can dismiss duplicates as they appear
questions.explanation: Feeding the previously reported findings into the re-run lets Claude diff against known issues and surface only new or still-unresolved ones, cutting the duplicate noise. Reducing or randomizing the scanned files just creates coverage gaps, and interactive mode is inappropriate for an automated nightly pipeline and doesn't solve the underlying duplication.

questions.stem: A CI-invoked Claude Code job reviews pull requests, but reviewers complain it ignores the team's mandatory testing standards, required fixtures, and severity criteria for what counts as a blocking issue. The prompt passed in the pipeline is short and generic. Where should these project-specific review conventions live so the CI-invoked Claude Code consistently applies them?
options: Put the testing standards, fixtures, and review criteria in CLAUDE.md so the CI-invoked Claude Code picks them up as project context
Store the conventions only in the reviewer's personal ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md file
Rely on Claude Code inferring the conventions from the code it reviews
Paste the full standards into each pipeline invocation's inline prompt every time a PR is reviewed
questions.explanation: CLAUDE.md is the mechanism for supplying durable project context (testing standards, fixtures, review criteria) to CI-invoked Claude Code, so it applies the conventions consistently without bloating every pipeline command. Inferring from the code is unreliable, cramming everything into each inline prompt is fragile and repetitive, and a personal user-level memory file is not shared via version control and won't be present in CI.

questions.task_desc: Explicit criteria for precision and false positive reduction
questions.stem: A customer-support triage prompt asks Claude to tag incoming tickets with a "churn_risk" label. The category is technically defined but Claude applies it to roughly 40% of tickets, including routine password resets, so the retention team no longer trusts any churn_risk tag. The team is still refining what churn signals actually matter and will take a few weeks to nail down precise criteria. The rest of the tagging (billing, bug, feature-request) is accurate and heavily relied upon. What is the best immediate action while the criteria are being improved?
options: Temporarily disable the churn_risk category so its false positives stop undermining trust in the tagging system, then re-enable it once precise criteria are defined
Lower the priority of churn_risk-tagged tickets so the noisy label has less operational impact while everything else continues
Keep churn_risk enabled but ask Claude to also output a confidence percentage for each churn_risk tag so the team can filter
Add "only apply churn_risk when you are highly confident" to the prompt so Claude self-limits its use of the label
questions.explanation: When one category produces high false positives, that noise undermines trust in the whole system, so the guidance is to temporarily disable that category while its criteria are improved rather than leave it emitting noise. Adding a "highly confident" or "be conservative" instruction is called out as ineffective for improving precision. Attaching a self-reported confidence score does not fix the underlying imprecise criteria and self-reported confidence is an unreliable proxy. Merely lowering priority leaves the untrustworthy label in place.

questions.task_desc: Explicit criteria for precision and false positive reduction
questions.stem: A Claude code-review prompt currently says: "Check that code comments are accurate and flag any that aren't." Reviewers complain it flags comments that are merely terse, outdated in wording, or stylistically different from the code, generating dozens of low-value nits per PR. The team only cares about one thing: comments that actively mislead. Which prompt revision will most reduce false positives?
options: Instruct Claude to flag a comment only when the behavior the comment claims contradicts what the code actually does
Instruct Claude to be more conservative and only flag comments it is confident are problematic
Instruct Claude to flag any comment that is not a word-for-word accurate description of the code it annotates
Instruct Claude to flag comments and assign each a severity score so reviewers can ignore the low-severity ones
questions.explanation: Replacing a vague instruction like "check comments are accurate" with a specific categorical criterion — flag only when the claimed behavior contradicts actual code behavior — directly targets the misleading cases and excludes terse-but-harmless comments. Telling the model to "be conservative" or "only when confident" does not improve precision. Emitting everything with a severity score still surfaces the noise. Requiring word-for-word accuracy is even stricter than the original and would increase false positives.

questions.task_desc: Explicit criteria for precision and false positive reduction
questions.stem: A structured-extraction pipeline uses Claude to review vendor contracts and flag "risky clauses." Different runs flag wildly different things as risky, and legal reviewers say the tool's notion of "risky" doesn't match theirs, producing many false positives on standard boilerplate. The team wants the flags to be consistent and to match how their lawyers actually triage. Which approach best establishes reliable, low-false-positive criteria?
options: Raise the bar in the prompt by telling the model to flag only clauses it considers genuinely high-risk
Define explicit severity tiers with concrete example clauses for each tier so the model classifies against named, illustrated criteria
Ask the model to explain its reasoning for each flag so reviewers can decide which ones to trust after the fact
Instruct the model to flag fewer clauses per contract to keep the total number of flags manageable
questions.explanation: Consistent, precise flagging comes from defining explicit severity criteria illustrated with concrete example clauses, so the model classifies against named, exemplified categories instead of an internal vague sense of "risky." Telling it to flag only "genuinely high-risk" clauses is the kind of vague high-confidence instruction the guidance says fails to improve precision. Capping the number of flags is an arbitrary quota unrelated to correctness. Post-hoc reasoning shifts the triage burden back to humans without making the criteria themselves consistent.

questions.task_desc: Few-shot prompting for consistency and quality
questions.stem: A customer support agent uses Claude to triage incoming tickets into routing categories. An architect has enumerated 12 explicit if/then rules in the prompt for known ticket types. The system works well for those types, but new product lines keep introducing ticket phrasings nobody anticipated, and the agent either forces them into a wrong category or fails. The architect keeps adding more explicit rules but can't keep pace. What approach best addresses the underlying problem?
options: Continue adding explicit if/then rules for each new ticket type as it appears, since categorical rules are always more precise than examples

Replace the exhaustive rule list with 2-4 few-shot examples that show the reasoning connecting a ticket's content to its category, so the model generalizes to novel phrasings rather than only matching pre-specified cases
Increase the number of enumerated rules to cover every conceivable phrasing before the new product lines launch
Add an instruction telling the model to 'be conservative and only categorize tickets you are highly confident about', routing the rest to a default queue
questions.explanation: Few-shot examples that demonstrate the reasoning behind a categorization let the model generalize to novel patterns it has never seen, which is exactly the failure mode here. Continuing to enumerate rules can never keep pace with unanticipated phrasings, and adding a vague 'be conservative / only high-confidence' instruction does not actually improve precision. Pre-specifying every possible phrasing is infeasible, which is why generalization from examples is the intended technique.

questions.task_desc: Few-shot prompting for consistency and quality
questions.stem: A structured extraction pipeline pulls vendor names, invoice totals, and due dates from scanned invoices. Reviewers notice that when a field is hard to read or genuinely absent, the model sometimes invents a plausible-looking value (a made-up vendor or a fabricated date) rather than leaving it out. The instructions already describe each field. Which change most directly reduces these fabricated values?
options: Add a stern instruction: 'Do not make up any values under any circumstances'
Raise the temperature so the model is less likely to commit to a single fabricated answer
Provide a few worked examples that include cases where a field is missing or illegible and show the correct handling, demonstrating the desired behavior when information is absent
Add a step that re-runs extraction three times and takes the majority value for each field
questions.explanation: Few-shot prompting reduces hallucination in extraction by demonstrating how to handle cases where information is absent or unreadable, giving the model a concrete pattern to follow instead of inventing values. Raising temperature increases variability and would make fabrication more likely, majority voting across runs does not help when every run fabricates, and a bare 'do not make things up' instruction is the kind of vague directive that does not reliably change behavior without demonstration.

questions.task_desc: Few-shot prompting for consistency and quality
questions.stem: A multi-agent code review system flags issues but developers complain the output is inconsistent: some findings are just a one-line label, others bury the fix in a paragraph, and reviewers can't tell why a given severity was assigned. The architect wants each finding to reliably include location, issue, severity, and a suggested fix, and wants the examples to also teach the model to justify its severity choices. How many examples should the architect provide and what should each example demonstrate?
options: A single example is enough, since one clear template fully constrains the format for all future findings
2-4 targeted examples, each showing the full output structure (location, issue, severity, suggested fix) and the reasoning for why that severity was chosen over the alternatives
10-15 examples covering as many distinct code patterns as possible, so the model has broad coverage even though it consumes more context
No examples; instead write increasingly detailed prose describing the desired format, since prose scales better than examples
questions.explanation: The guidance calls for 2-4 targeted few-shot examples, each demonstrating the desired output format (location, issue, severity, suggested fix) and the reasoning for why one choice was made over alternatives. A single example under-specifies the range of cases, a large batch of examples wastes context without proportional benefit, and relying on ever-more-detailed prose is precisely the situation where prose is interpreted inconsistently and examples are the more effective tool.

questions.task_desc: Structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
questions.stem: Your team migrated an invoice-extraction pipeline to Claude tool_use with a strict JSON schema. Downstream parsing errors dropped to zero, but the finance team still reports problems: on several invoices the sum of the extracted line-item amounts does not match the extracted invoice total, and occasionally a tax amount lands in the shipping field. A stakeholder concludes that the schema itself must be malformed. What is the most accurate assessment?
options: The problem is that tool_choice is set to "auto"; forcing the specific extraction tool would make the arithmetic and field assignments correct
The schema is malformed; a correct JSON schema would enforce that line items sum to the total and that each value matches its semantic field

Tool_use with a JSON schema guarantees only syntactic validity; semantic errors like line items not summing or values placed in the wrong field are not prevented and require additional validation logic
Switching from tool_use back to a JSON code block in the prompt would let the model reason more freely and fix the summing errors
questions.explanation: Tool_use with a JSON schema guarantees the output is schema-compliant and free of JSON syntax errors, but it cannot enforce semantic correctness such as line items summing to a total or values being placed in the correct field. Those semantic errors need separate validation (for example, extracting a calculated total alongside a stated total). A JSON schema cannot express cross-field arithmetic constraints, so calling the schema malformed misdiagnoses the issue. tool_choice controls whether a tool is called, not the correctness of its contents, and reverting to a prompted code block would reintroduce syntax errors without fixing semantics.

questions.task_desc: Structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
questions.stem: You are designing the JSON schema for a tool that extracts contact details (email, phone, mailing address) from scanned business documents. Many documents legitimately omit some of these fields. In early testing you notice the model invents plausible-looking phone numbers and addresses when the document does not contain them. How should you design the schema to reduce this fabrication?
options: Mark every field as required so the model must locate each value and cannot skip the hard ones
Add a post-processing rule that deletes any field whose value the model flags as low-confidence
Make fields optional or nullable so the model can omit or return null when the source lacks that information, rather than being pressured to fill every required field
Raise the model temperature so it produces more varied outputs and is less likely to repeat fabricated values
questions.explanation: When source documents may lack certain information, designing fields as optional or nullable lets the model omit or null them instead of fabricating values to satisfy a required field. Making every field required does the opposite, pressuring the model to invent data. A low-confidence deletion pass is a downstream patch that does not address the incentive to fabricate at extraction time, and raising temperature increases, not decreases, fabrication.

questions.task_desc: Structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
questions.stem: Your structured-extraction tool classifies each support ticket into a category and also records the primary product mentioned. Some tickets are genuinely ambiguous about their category, and occasionally a ticket references a product outside your known catalog. You want the schema to handle both situations gracefully instead of forcing a wrong pick. Which schema design best fits the guidance?
options: Make the category and product fields free-text with no enum, so the model can write whatever it wants for any case
Add an enum value like "unclear" for ambiguous category cases, and an "other" enum value paired with a free-text detail field for products outside the known set
Add a top-level confidence score and route anything below a threshold to a human, leaving the enums strict
Remove the ambiguous tickets from the batch before extraction so the schema only ever sees clear-cut cases
questions.explanation: The guidance is to use an enum value such as "unclear" for ambiguous cases and an "other" value plus a detail field for extensibility beyond a known set. This keeps the output structured while handling both ambiguity and novel values. Dropping to free-text everywhere abandons the reliability of enums, a confidence threshold does not give the model a way to represent ambiguity within the field itself, and pre-filtering ambiguous tickets sidesteps rather than solves the extraction requirement.

questions.stem: Your team runs two separate extraction pipelines against Claude with tool_use. Pipeline A extracts `policy_number` from insurance PDFs; about 6% of runs return values that fail a regex format check (extra whitespace, transposed segment order). Pipeline B extracts `beneficiary_ssn` from the same PDFs; about 6% return null because many of these particular policies genuinely omit the SSN. Both teams want to add a retry-with-error-feedback loop that re-sends the document plus the validation error. For which pipeline will this loop actually reduce the failure rate?
options: Pipeline B only, because null values indicate Claude was uncertain and a second attempt with the error attached will surface the value it missed
Neither pipeline, because tool_use already guarantees schema-compliant output so a retry loop can never change the result
Both pipelines equally, because retry-with-error-feedback improves any field where the first attempt did not satisfy validation

Pipeline A only, because the failures are format/structural errors that error feedback can guide Claude to correct, whereas Pipeline B's failures stem from information genuinely absent in the source, which retries cannot recover
questions.explanation: Retry-with-error-feedback fixes format and structural mistakes because the correct value exists in the source and the error steers Claude toward it; the whitespace and segment-order failures are exactly this recoverable case. When a field is null because the value is genuinely absent from the document, no amount of re-prompting can recover data that isn't there, so retrying that pipeline just wastes calls. It is not true that retries help any failed field, and tool_use only eliminates JSON syntax errors, not semantic or missing-data outcomes.

questions.stem: A contract-total extraction pipeline keeps producing invoices where the sum of line items does not match the extracted total, and downstream validation rejects them. Rather than only retrying after a rejection, an architect wants to redesign the extraction so Claude helps catch these mismatches itself as part of the initial extraction. Which schema design best supports this self-correction goal?
options: Set `tool_choice` to forced so Claude must call the extraction tool, guaranteeing the totals always match
Add a single free-text `notes` field and instruct Claude to mention any discrepancies it noticed
Have Claude extract `calculated_total` (summed from the line items) alongside `stated_total` (read from the document) and include a `conflict_detected` boolean the pipeline can act on
Lower the temperature and add a stricter regex to the `total_amount` field so malformed totals are rejected before they reach validation
questions.explanation: A self-correction flow works by having Claude produce both a computed value and the value stated in the document, plus an explicit conflict flag, so the mismatch is surfaced structurally and the pipeline can route or re-examine it. A free-text notes field is inconsistent and not machine-actionable, forced tool_choice only guarantees the tool runs (not that totals reconcile), and a regex only catches malformed strings, not two well-formed numbers that disagree.

questions.stem: A customer support platform runs an agentic loop where Claude answers a ticket, calls a lookup_order tool, receives the result, then calls a refund_status tool before composing a final reply. To cut costs, an engineer wants to move this entire per-ticket workflow onto the Message Batches API, submitting all of a day's tickets in one nightly batch. Assuming latency is genuinely not a concern, what is the fundamental blocker with this plan?
options: The Batches API charges the full standard rate once tool_use is involved, eliminating the 50% savings that motivated the move
The Batches API cannot use tools at all, so any workflow that invokes lookup_order or refund_status must run through the standard Messages API

The Batches API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request, so an interactive loop that calls a tool, reads the result, and then calls another tool cannot execute as one batch request
The Batches API caps each request at a single tool call, so the workflow would need to be split into exactly two batches
questions.explanation: The batch API is designed for single-shot requests and does not support the interactive multi-turn tool-calling loop where the model calls a tool, receives the result, and then decides on a next tool call within the same request. That agentic back-and-forth is the blocker here, not tool availability, a per-request tool-count cap, or a pricing change. The ~50% discount applies regardless of tool use, and there is no rule limiting a request to one tool call or forbidding tools entirely.

questions.stem: A data team submits a batch of 20,000 documents for structured extraction on Friday afternoon and tells stakeholders results will 'definitely be ready in an hour, since it's cheaper we can just poll.' Which characteristic of the Message Batches API makes this expectation unsafe?
options: Results are only released at fixed midnight boundaries, so anything submitted after noon waits until the next day
The Batches API only returns results after all requests across all customers in the region complete
The Batches API provides up to a 24-hour processing window with no guaranteed latency SLA, so results may not be ready in any specific short timeframe
Batches are processed in strict FIFO order, so a large queue ahead of yours can indefinitely delay results
questions.explanation: The batch API offers cost savings in exchange for a processing window of up to 24 hours with no guaranteed latency SLA, so promising completion within an hour is unsound. The other explanations invent mechanisms the guide does not describe: there is no documented strict cross-customer FIFO queue, no all-customers-must-finish gating, and no fixed midnight release boundary. The correct concern is simply the absence of a latency guarantee within the 24-hour window.

questions.stem: An operations team has an internal SLA promising analysts that any document submitted will have results available within 30 hours. They want to use the Message Batches API (up to a 24-hour processing window) and are deciding how often to kick off a batch of whatever documents have accumulated. What submission cadence reliably satisfies the 30-hour SLA?
options: Submit continuously in real time, since the 24-hour window applies per document rather than per batch
Submit every 6 hours, so any document waits at most 6 hours before submission and then up to 24 hours to process, totaling at most 30 hours
Submit once every 30 hours, matching the batch interval to the SLA exactly
Submit only when at least 10,000 documents have accumulated, regardless of elapsed time, to maximize the cost discount
questions.explanation: Because a document can wait in the accumulation buffer before a batch is kicked off and then take up to 24 hours to process, the SLA must cover both parts: submitting every 6 hours bounds the wait at 6 hours plus 24 hours of processing, exactly 30 hours. Submitting every 30 hours ignores the 24-hour processing time and would blow the SLA. Waiting for a document count gives no time bound at all, and real-time submission misunderstands the window, which applies to processing the batch, not per-document arrival.

questions.stem: You operate a two-stage extraction-and-review system: a review instance re-checks each extracted invoice for correctness. Volume is high and you cannot send every document to a human. You want to concentrate human attention where the automated review is least sure. Which design makes best use of the review pass for this routing goal?
options: Have each verification pass self-report a confidence level so results can be routed for calibrated human review of low-confidence cases
Route documents to humans based on the sentiment of the review instance's free-text summary
Send a fixed 50% of all reviewed documents to humans regardless of the review outcome
Trust the review pass's binary pass/fail flag alone and send only the 'fail' documents to humans
questions.explanation: Verification passes can self-report a confidence level, and that calibrated confidence is what lets you route low-confidence cases to human review rather than reviewing everything. Sentiment of a free-text summary is an unreliable proxy, a fixed percentage ignores which cases are actually uncertain, and a bare pass/fail flag discards the graded confidence signal needed for calibrated routing.

questions.stem: A team is building a customer support agent that runs long sessions. To stay within the context window, an engineer proposes automatically summarizing older turns into a running natural-language recap (e.g., "customer had a billing issue and we discussed a refund") and dropping the original messages. During review, an architect warns this design will systematically degrade reliability on disputes. Which specific failure does progressive summarization most directly introduce?
options: It causes the model to lose the ability to call tools because tool definitions are stripped out of the summary
It prevents the agent from ever escalating to a human because the escalation triggers are summarized away
It permanently increases per-request cost because summaries must be regenerated on every turn
It condenses precise transactional details like amounts, percentages, dates, and customer-stated expectations into vague paraphrases that can no longer be recovered
questions.explanation: The documented risk of progressive summarization is that it condenses numbers, percentages, dates, and customer-stated expectations into vagueness, which is exactly what breaks disputes that hinge on precise facts. Tool definitions live outside the conversation history and are not affected by summarizing turns, so losing tool-calling ability is not the concern. Regenerating summaries is a minor cost detail, not the reliability failure being warned about, and escalation logic is driven by triggers rather than removed by summarization.

questions.stem: A Claude Code agent debugging a production incident calls tools that each return large payloads: a full 4,000-line log dump, a complete database row with 60 columns, and a verbose API response. Over 15 turns these raw outputs accumulate in the conversation and the agent starts giving inconsistent answers. A reliability-minded design would address the raw tool output how?
options: Move all tool outputs to the very beginning of the context so the model reads them first
Disable the tools after the first call so they cannot return large payloads again
Summarize the entire conversation, including tool outputs, into a single running natural-language recap each turn
Trim each verbose tool output down to the relevant fields before it accumulates in the conversation history
questions.explanation: The guidance is to trim verbose tool outputs to only the relevant fields before they pile up, keeping the context lean without discarding meaningful information. Disabling the tools would block legitimate future calls and does not address the payloads already accumulated. Summarizing everything into prose reintroduces the loss-of-precision problem, and relocating outputs to the front does not reduce the accumulating volume that is diluting the context.

questions.stem: An engineer optimizing token usage in a multi-turn support agent decides that on each new API request they will send only the latest user message plus a short summary, omitting the full prior message history to save tokens. Subsequent responses start contradicting decisions the agent made earlier in the same session. Per context-management best practice, what should the design do instead?
options: Send only the most recent three turns, since the 'lost in the middle' effect makes older turns unreliable anyway
Rely on the model's internal memory of the session so history never needs to be re-sent
Pass the complete conversation history in each subsequent request so the model maintains coherence across turns
Replace the history entirely with a single confidence score summarizing the session so far
questions.explanation: Coherence across turns depends on passing the complete conversation history in each subsequent request; the model is stateless between requests and cannot recall a session on its own. Truncating to the last few turns drops earlier commitments and produces exactly the contradictions described. A confidence score carries none of the substantive context needed to stay consistent, and there is no persistent internal session memory to rely on.

questions.stem: A team building a returns-support agent wants to automate escalation to a human specialist. An engineer proposes: "Have the model output a self-reported confidence score from 0 to 1 on each turn, plus run a sentiment classifier on the customer's messages. Escalate whenever confidence drops below 0.6 or sentiment turns negative." What is the most significant flaw in this escalation design per best practice?
options: The confidence threshold of 0.6 is too low and will cause the agent to escalate the majority of routine returns unnecessarily
Sentiment analysis is out of scope for the agent and should be handled by a separate dedicated monitoring service instead
Two independent escalation signals conflict with each other, so the design should pick either confidence OR sentiment but not combine them

Self-reported confidence and sentiment are unreliable proxies for case complexity; escalation should be driven by concrete triggers like policy gaps, explicit human requests, and inability to make progress
questions.explanation: The guide explicitly states that sentiment-based escalation and self-reported confidence scores are unreliable proxies for case complexity. Sound escalation is driven by concrete triggers: explicit human requests, policy exceptions or gaps, and inability to make meaningful progress. The threshold value, service architecture, and combining-signals framings all miss that the underlying signals themselves are the wrong basis for the decision.

questions.stem: A support agent looks up a customer by name and the account system returns three different customers who all match "J. Martinez." The agent must proceed to process an address change. Which behavior best follows escalation and ambiguity-resolution guidance?
options: Proceed with the first match returned and let the customer correct it if the address change lands on the wrong account
Immediately escalate the conversation to a human agent because multiple matches indicate a data-quality problem
Ask the customer for an additional identifier (such as account number, email, or ZIP) to disambiguate before proceeding
Pick the account with the most recent activity, since that is statistically the most likely person contacting support
questions.explanation: When a lookup returns multiple customer matches, the correct move is to clarify by asking for additional identifiers rather than guessing. Choosing by recency or taking the first result is exactly the heuristic selection the guide warns against, and immediate escalation is unwarranted because the agent can resolve the ambiguity itself simply by asking.

questions.stem: A customer messages an insurance-support agent: "This is the third time I've called about my delayed claim and I'm really upset." The agent can see the claim status and is able to expedite it using an available tool. Which response pattern is most aligned with the guidance for handling frustration?
options: Acknowledge the customer's frustration and offer to resolve the issue since it is within the agent's capability, escalating only if the customer then reiterates a desire for a human
Escalate to a human specialist right away, because a customer expressing being upset is a reliable signal that the case exceeds the agent's scope
Skip acknowledging the frustration and immediately expedite the claim, since resolving the problem quickly matters more than commentary
Ask the customer to confirm they are frustrated before deciding whether to continue or route the conversation to a human
questions.explanation: The guide says to acknowledge frustration while offering resolution when the issue is within capability, and to escalate only if the customer reiterates. Escalating on the expression of being upset treats sentiment as a complexity proxy, which is unreliable. Ignoring the emotion entirely misses the acknowledgment step, and asking the customer to confirm their frustration is unnecessary and unhelpful.

questions.stem: A multi-agent research system dispatches five subagents to query different internal knowledge bases. One subagent's query to the 'legal-precedents' store returns zero rows because that store genuinely contains no documents matching the topic. A second subagent's query to the 'financials' store also returns zero rows, but only because its database connection was refused. Both subagents report the same payload back to the coordinator: `{results: []}`. Why is this reporting scheme a reliability problem?
options: The coordinator cannot distinguish a valid empty result (a successful query that legitimately found no matches) from an access failure that needs a retry, so it may treat missing data as confirmed absence of data
Zero-row responses should always trigger an immediate retry, so the coordinator will waste resources retrying the legitimately empty legal-precedents query
Empty result sets are inherently invalid output, so both subagents should have raised an exception that terminates the whole workflow
The subagents should have merged their results before reporting, so the coordinator is receiving too many separate payloads to reconcile
questions.explanation: The core failure is that an identical empty-results payload conflates two fundamentally different outcomes: a successful query that found nothing versus a connection failure where the query never really ran. The coordinator needs distinct signals so it retries the access failure but trusts the legitimate empty result. Blanket-retrying every zero-row response is wrong because a genuinely empty store should be reported as no matches, not retried endlessly. Merging results does not address the missing failure-type distinction, and treating all empty results as exceptions that kill the workflow is itself an anti-pattern.

questions.stem: In a hub-and-spoke research system, the coordinator fans out to eight subagents. One subagent hits a transient timeout on a single upstream API. The current implementation catches this and aborts the entire run, returning a top-level 'search unavailable' error to the user, discarding the seven subagents that completed successfully. The team wants to improve resilience. Which redesign best matches the guide's guidance on error propagation?
options: Have the coordinator strip all error handling from subagents so every failure surfaces identically at the top level for a single retry decision

Attempt local recovery for the transient failure inside the subagent, and if it still cannot be resolved, propagate structured error context alongside the partial results from the seven successful subagents, with the synthesis annotated to note the coverage gap
Replace the timeout handling with a rule that returns an empty result set marked as success so the workflow always completes without surfacing errors
Configure the coordinator to terminate on the first subagent failure but log the error so it can be reviewed after the run
questions.explanation: The guide favors local recovery in subagents for transient failures, propagating only what cannot be resolved, and always preserving partial results rather than discarding successful work. The synthesis should be annotated with the coverage gap so the missing section is visible. Terminating the whole workflow on a single failure is explicitly an anti-pattern, whether or not it is logged afterward. Returning empty-as-success silently suppresses the error, another named anti-pattern, and stripping subagent error handling removes the local-recovery opportunity entirely.

questions.stem: A subagent responsible for querying a product-catalog service fails partway through a multi-agent pricing analysis. The engineer designing the failure response wants the coordinator to be able to make an intelligent recovery decision (retry, route around, or degrade gracefully) rather than just aborting. Which failure payload from the subagent best supports that goal?
options: A generic string, 'catalog search unavailable,' so the coordinator immediately knows to stop
Structured error context including the failure type, the attempted query, any partial results already retrieved, and suggested alternatives
The raw stack trace from the underlying service exception, passed through verbatim to the coordinator
A boolean `failed: true` flag with no additional detail, keeping the payload minimal
questions.explanation: Intelligent coordinator recovery depends on rich, structured context: what kind of failure occurred, what was attempted, what partial results already exist, and what alternatives might work. A generic unavailable message hides exactly the context needed to decide between retrying, routing around, or degrading gracefully, and a bare boolean flag is even less informative. A raw stack trace is unstructured and does not surface the attempted query, partial results, or alternatives in a form the coordinator can act on.

questions.task_desc: Context management in large codebase exploration
questions.stem: A coordinator agent is mapping a large monorepo. To answer one architectural question it must trace how a request flows through 40+ files across three services, each read producing hundreds of lines of source that the coordinator only needs a one-line summary of. Early experiments show that after the coordinator directly reads all 40 files, its main context is so full of raw source that it starts giving vague, 'typical layered architecture' style answers instead of citing the actual handlers it found. Which architectural change best preserves the coordinator's ability to reason precisely?
options: Delegate the file-by-file tracing to subagents that each explore a service and return only a distilled summary, keeping the verbose source out of the coordinator's context
Have the coordinator read all 40 files up front in a single batch so everything is available before it begins reasoning
Instruct the coordinator in its system prompt to 'be concise and avoid generic descriptions' so it stops referencing typical patterns
Increase the coordinator's iteration cap so it has more turns to re-read files it has forgotten
questions.explanation: The right move is to isolate the verbose exploration in subagents that return distilled summaries, so the coordinator's context holds findings rather than raw source and it can keep reasoning precisely. Raising an iteration cap does not address the root cause (context saturation) and re-reading only refills context with more source. A 'be concise' instruction is a prompt-level plea that does nothing about the underlying context degradation. Reading everything up front makes saturation worse, which is exactly what caused the generic answers.

questions.task_desc: Context management in large codebase exploration
questions.stem: An overnight agent is doing a multi-hour audit of a 300k-line codebase, exporting an evolving dependency map and vulnerability list. Halfway through, the machine reboots and the process dies. When you restart, you want the agent to resume where it left off rather than re-explore everything, and you want each independent exploration unit's findings to be recoverable. Which design best supports crash recovery here?
options: Turn up progressive summarization frequency so the running context stays small enough to survive a reboot in memory
Have each agent export its state to a known location and write a manifest that the coordinator loads on resume to reconstruct what was already discovered
Rely on `--resume` with the session name alone, since named session resumption automatically restores all in-progress tool results and findings
Keep all findings in the live conversation history and re-run the full exploration from scratch after any crash, trusting the model to be faster the second time
questions.explanation: Structured state persistence is the fit: each agent exports its state to a known location and the coordinator loads a manifest on resume to reconstruct prior discoveries. Named session resumption does not durably capture in-progress findings across a hard crash, and the agent must even be told about changed files on resume. Summarization frequency addresses context size, not durability across a process death, and risks condensing key facts. Re-running from scratch defeats the recovery goal entirely.

questions.task_desc: Context management in large codebase exploration
questions.stem: A developer is deep into a single long-running Claude Code session exploring an unfamiliar codebase. They have already extracted and saved the important findings (key class names, the auth flow, a dependency sketch) into a scratchpad file, but the running conversation is now bloated with dozens of full file dumps and search outputs, and responses are getting less specific. They want to keep the same session going for the next phase without losing the findings they care about. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Start an entirely new session and paste the raw file dumps back in so no exploration detail is lost
Do nothing about context and simply raise a safety iteration cap so the session can run longer before stopping
Fork the session so the bloated context is branched away and continue exploring on the fork
Use `/compact` to reduce the accumulated context during the extended exploration, continuing the session with the scratchpad findings still available
questions.explanation: For reducing accumulated context within an ongoing extended exploration, `/compact` is the intended tool, and because the durable findings already live in a scratchpad file they remain available afterward. Forking creates a divergent branch from a baseline rather than trimming the current session's bloat. An iteration cap is only a safety guardrail and does nothing about the context saturation causing vaguer answers. Starting fresh and re-pasting raw dumps just re-introduces the same verbose material that caused the problem.

questions.task_desc: Human review workflows and confidence calibration
questions.stem: A team runs a document extraction pipeline that flags roughly 8% of extractions as low-confidence for human review. Reviewers rarely find errors in the remaining 92% that auto-proceed, so leadership wants to shrink the review queue. Before doing so, an architect insists on adding a process where a small percentage of the auto-proceeding, high-confidence extractions are randomly pulled and re-checked by humans across each document type. Why is this the right safeguard to add?
options: Stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions measures the true error rate in the auto-proceeding population and can surface novel error patterns that the confidence signal misses
Sampling the high-confidence set is only useful for estimating reviewer throughput, not extraction quality, since those items were already deemed reliable
Re-checking a sample of high-confidence extractions retrains the model on its mistakes, so the confidence threshold self-corrects over time
It lets the team raise the confidence threshold so that fewer items are ever flagged, directly reducing the review queue as leadership wants
questions.explanation: Errors that slip through are precisely the ones the confidence signal failed to flag, so you can only detect them by auditing the auto-proceeding population itself. Stratified random sampling across document types measures the real error rate in that high-confidence segment and can reveal novel failure modes. The claim about retraining is out of scope and not how sampling works; treating the sample as only a throughput measure ignores its quality-monitoring purpose; and raising the threshold does not tell you whether the auto-approved items are actually correct.

questions.task_desc: Human review workflows and confidence calibration
questions.stem: An engineer wants low-confidence extractions routed to reviewers and high-confidence ones auto-approved, using per-field confidence scores. They plan to pick the routing threshold by eyeballing a few example outputs and choosing a number that 'feels safe.' What does the guide say is required to make field-level confidence scores a trustworthy routing signal?
options: Setting the threshold high enough (e.g., 0.95) that only near-certain fields auto-approve, which makes calibration unnecessary
Asking the model to also state, in natural language, how sure it is, and routing based on that stated certainty
Averaging the field-level scores into a single per-document confidence value before applying the threshold
Calibrating the field-level confidence scores against a labeled validation set so the scores map to actual observed accuracy
questions.explanation: Confidence scores are only meaningful for routing once they have been calibrated against a labeled validation set, so a given score corresponds to a known real-world accuracy. Averaging fields into one number discards the field-level granularity the guide relies on; picking a high round number by feel is exactly the uncalibrated guess being warned against; and the model's stated, self-reported certainty is an unreliable proxy.

questions.stem: A market-research agent aggregates competitor pricing from three subagents. Subagent A reports the target company's annual recurring revenue as $2.1B, subagent B reports $1.8B, and subagent C reports $2.4B. The coordinator must produce a single briefing. Which approach best handles this discrepancy?
options: Retry all three subagents with a lower temperature until they converge on the same number before writing the briefing
Have the coordinator average the three figures and report the single mean value to avoid confusing the reader with conflicting numbers
Preserve all three figures in the briefing, each annotated with its source attribution, and let the coordinator surface the conflict for reconciliation rather than silently picking one
Instruct the coordinator to select the value reported by the subagent that searched the most authoritative-sounding source and discard the others
questions.explanation: When statistics conflict, the guide says to annotate the conflicting values with their source attribution and let the coordinator reconcile, rather than arbitrarily selecting one value. Averaging invents a figure no source supports and hides the disagreement; picking the most 'authoritative-sounding' source is an arbitrary selection the guide warns against; retrying to force convergence discards real differences that may stem from different collection dates or definitions. Surfacing the conflict with provenance lets a human or the coordinator judge which figure applies.

questions.stem: A competitive-intelligence agent flags a 'data integrity problem': one subagent reports global EV market share at 14% while another reports 18%, and the agent treats these as contradictory and demands human review. On inspection, one figure is from a 2022 report and the other from a 2024 report. What should the pipeline require of subagents to prevent this false-contradiction pattern?
options: Require subagents to attach a self-reported confidence score to each statistic so low-confidence values can be filtered out
Require subagents to round all statistics to the nearest 5% so minor differences collapse into agreement
Require the coordinator to always keep only the most recent statistic and drop any older figure automatically
Require each subagent to emit the publication or collection date alongside every statistic so temporal differences aren't misread as contradictions
questions.explanation: The guide specifically calls for requiring publication or collection dates so that temporal differences between statistics are not read as contradictions. Rounding would corrupt the data and still wouldn't explain a 4-point gap; auto-dropping older figures loses potentially relevant historical context and is a form of arbitrary selection; self-reported confidence scores are called out elsewhere as unreliable proxies and don't address the root cause, which is a missing time dimension. Dates let the coordinator recognize both figures as valid for their respective years.

questions.stem: A research agent produces a single report that mixes a company's quarterly financial figures, a summary of recent regulatory news, and a set of API integration steps. The team complains the report is hard to consume because everything is written as dense uniform paragraphs. What guidance best addresses this?
options: Split the report into three separate agent sessions so each content type never appears together
Render each content type in the form that fits it — financial data as tables, news as prose, technical steps as lists — rather than forcing one uniform format
Route the whole report to human review because mixed content types indicate the extraction was low-confidence
Convert the entire report into a single flat bulleted list so every item has consistent visual weight
questions.explanation: The guide states that content types should be rendered appropriately — financial data as tables, news as prose, technical material as lists — not forced into a single uniform format. A flat bulleted list is itself a uniform format and would make financial data harder to read, not easier. Mixed content types are a presentation concern, not a signal of low extraction confidence, so routing everything to human review misreads the problem; splitting into separate sessions is unnecessary and doesn't address how each section is formatted.
